why: If your agents accumulate million-token trajectories, this paper argues you can push RL post-training past 2M tokens on a fixed, commodity GPU budget instead of leaning on length generalization at deployment.
notes: The problem framing: inference is approaching million-token contexts while RL post-training often stays at 256K tokens or below, forcing reliance on length generalization at deployment; this matters most for agents whose observations, tool outputs, documents, and prior decisions accumulate over long trajectories.

Core mechanism (three parts): evaluate the shared prompt without autograd, retain only model-specific state needed by later tokens, and replay short response branches one at a time; this shrinks the live training graph in exchange for additional replay time. Instantiated with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).

On 8x H20 GPUs with the hybrid recurrent/full-attention Qwen3.6-27B: completes grouped scoring and response backward at 2.1M positions for groups of 2 and 8; scaling the group from 2 to 8 adds only 0.21 GB peak allocated memory. A separate stress test on the same setup reaches 4.46M positions.
On 32x H20 GPUs: validates the end-to-end execution path for a 2.1M-token prompt across all 78 layers of the compressed-attention MoE GLM-5.2.

Explicit caveat from the authors: results establish execution capacity, not complete training correctness, because captured prompt state is detached and some distributed forward and gradient composition paths remain incomplete.
summary: LongStraw is an architecture-aware execution stack for million-token RL post-training under a fixed GPU budget, instantiated with GRPO. It addresses the gap between inference systems nearing million-token contexts and RL post-training that typically remains at 256K tokens or below. The method evaluates the shared prompt without autograd, retains only model-specific state needed by later tokens, and replays short response branches sequentially, trading extra replay time for a smaller live training graph. Reported results include grouped scoring and response backward at 2.1M positions on 8 H20 GPUs for the Qwen3.6-27B hybrid recurrent/full-attention model (with a 4.46M-position stress test), and an end-to-end execution path validated for a 2.1M-token prompt across all 78 layers of the compressed-attention MoE GLM-5.2 on 32 H20 GPUs. The authors are explicit that these experiments demonstrate execution capacity rather than training correctness, since the captured prompt state is detached and some distributed forward and gradient composition paths remain incomplete.
novelty: First concrete systems paper demonstrating RL post-training execution (GRPO) beyond 2M tokens on commodity GPU budgets, via no-autograd prompt evaluation + selective state retention + sequential response replay
audience: ML infrastructure engineers and researchers closing the gap between long-context inference and RL post-training

title: Kimi K3, and what we can still learn from the pelican benchmark
why: A Chinese lab shipping a 2.8T-parameter open-weight model at Sonnet-tier pricing signals that the open frontier is now competing on capability rather than undercutting on cost, and Willison's throwaway pelican test surfaces a real deployment gotcha: a single fixed "max" reasoning level.
notes: Moonshot AI's Kimi K3 is self-described as 2.8 trillion parameters, billed as the first 'open 3T-class model' (rounding up), with open weights promised by July 27, 2026; it more than doubles DeepSeek's 1.6T V4 Pro.
Pricing is $3/M input and $15/M output tokens, matching Anthropic's Claude Sonnet tier and, per the article, the most expensive model yet from a Chinese lab, a large jump from K2.6's $0.95/$4.

Third-party Artificial Analysis figures cited: long-horizon knowledge-work Elo of 1547 (+732 over K2.6, behind only Claude Fable 5), cost per task $0.94 vs GPT-5.6 Sol $1.04 and Opus 4.8 $1.80, and 21% fewer output tokens than K2.6; K3 also leads Arena.ai's Frontend Code arena. Vendor self-reported benchmarks show K3 mostly beating Claude Opus 4.8 max and GPT-5.5 high but losing to Claude Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol.
K3 exposes only one reasoning effort level, 'max'; Willison's pelican SVG prompt burned 13,241 reasoning tokens for a 3,417-token response, costing 25 cents for one trivial request.

Token accounting oddity: the 5-word pelican prompt counted as 95 input tokens and 'hi' as 86, leading Willison to infer a roughly 85-token hidden system prompt the model refused to leak; vision input worked well, producing accurate alt text for 0.6 cents.

Willison argues his 21-month-old pelican-on-a-bicycle test has largely lost its correlation to model quality (GLM-5.2 out-draws Fable 5) and never touched agentic tool-calling, but still values it as a forcing function to actually run each model, a cost/reasoning sanity check, and SVG/spatial-reasoning confirmation for small local models.
summary: Moonshot AI announced Kimi K3, a 2.8-trillion-parameter model it calls the first "open 3T-class" release, available now via web and API with open weights promised by July 27, 2026. It is priced at $3/M input and $15/M output tokens, matching Claude Sonnet's tier and, per Simon Willison, the most expensive model from a Chinese lab, versus K2.6's $0.95/$4. Cited Artificial Analysis figures include a long-horizon knowledge-work Elo of 1547 (+732 over K2.6, behind only Claude Fable 5), a $0.94 cost per task, 21% fewer output tokens than K2.6, and a lead on Arena.ai's Frontend Code arena; Moonshot's own benchmarks put K3 above Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5 but below Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol. Running his SVG "pelican riding a bicycle" test, Willison found K3 exposes only one reasoning level, "max," spending 13,241 reasoning tokens and 25 cents on a single trivial prompt, and inferred a roughly 85-token hidden system prompt from token counts; vision input produced strong alt text. He argues the pelican benchmark has lost most of its correlation to real capability and ignores agentic tool-calling, but still uses it as a forcing function to try models and gauge cost, reasoning, and basic SVG/spatial competence.
novelty: First open-weight model at 2.8T parameters (eclipsing DeepSeek V4 Pro's 1.6T); Chinese lab pricing jumping to Claude Sonnet-tier for the first time; observation that K3 has only one reasoning effort level ("max") with severe cost implications
audience: ML engineers evaluating frontier model tradeoffs for cost-sensitive agentic workloads

title: Show HN: Sentinel – open-source QA agent that reads your code before it clicks
why: A working QA agent that reads your repo to derive business flows before touching a browser, and verifies backend state per step, is a more honest template for autonomous testing than the click-around demos, and it is open source under MIT.
notes: Architecture: deterministic recon (grep/find, zero model calls) extracts frontend routes, API modules, services, and DB entities into a digest; the model reasons over that to derive prioritized end-to-end flows, cached per commit. From a hotel PMS repo plus admin creds and no test plan, it derived nine critical business flows on its own.

Backend truth-checking: a first-class api_request tool runs fetch inside the page, replaying the Authorization header the frontend already sent (no token reconstruction). This caught bugs invisible from the UI: a confirm returning NO_AVAILABILITY on a reservation that already held its room, calendar/API disagreement, and a check-in returning 200 while registrationStatus stayed NONE server-side.

Safety rails: the model only ever gets browser and API tools (never shell or filesystem); a hard budget stops all action tools at 90 tool calls, leaving only finish. Non-determinism is handled by running each flow twice by default (FLOW_ATTEMPTS) and unioning findings, motivated by one flow where one attempt found zero bugs and another found five.

Web3 access: Sentinel injects its own window.ethereum (or EIP-6963) implementation backed by an unfunded burner key kept in Node; any method not on an explicit read-only allowlist is refused, send/broadcast methods never reach the network, and a key that has ever been used aborts the run. An adversarial pass found the initial balance check failed open on lookup errors and was fixed to fail closed.

Cost/results claims: deepest hotel PMS run was 364 steps for $1.95; a 61-step perpetuals-exchange frontend run cost $0.28 and surfaced 9 functional bugs plus 13 design findings with no transaction reaching chain. Models are Xiaomi Mimo via the pi harness: mimo-v2.5-pro (text-only) for decisions, mimo-v2-omni (multimodal, needed 6,000 max tokens to avoid JSON truncation) for vision grading; Claude and codex/gpt-5.5 used only outside the hot loop.

Caveats stated by the author: no field benchmark of models (Mimo is default because it was already configured, not proven optimal); exploration varies run to run; write-capable QA should never touch production data; wallet apps are tested only up to the moment of settlement, and actually filling a trade on an anvil fork is unbuilt. Sending code/DOM/diffs to a model is opt-in per target via ai_allowed.
summary: Sentinel is an MIT-licensed, open-source QA agent that reads a codebase before driving a browser: a deterministic grep/find recon pass digests routes, API modules, services, and DB entities, then a model derives a prioritized list of end-to-end business flows with UI steps and backend assertions, cached per commit. It runs each flow as an agent loop where Playwright drives the browser and an api_request tool checks server state by replaying the frontend's own Authorization header, plus a multimodal vision pass grades each screen. The author reports that, given only a hotel PMS repo and disposable admin credentials, it independently derived nine business flows and caught backend-only bugs (a NO_AVAILABILITY state-machine bug, calendar/API disagreement, a half-completed check-in transition). For web3 apps it injects a Node-held burner-wallet provider with send methods blocked by an allowlist, and reports a 61-step perpetuals-exchange run costing $0.28 that found 9 functional and 13 design issues with no on-chain transaction. It runs on Mimo (Xiaomi) models by default through the pi harness but is provider-agnostic; the author explicitly notes he has not benchmarked models and wants field reports on bugs-found-per-dollar. Stated limits include run-to-run variance (mitigated by running each flow twice), setup complexity for booting stacks, and that wallet testing stops before settlement.
novelty: Code-reading-before-clicking: deterministic recon extracts repo structure so model derives prioritized business flows autonomously before any browser interaction; web3 wallet injection via window.ethereum stub kept entirely in Node with send methods blocked by allowlist
audience: Full-stack JS engineers wanting automated end-to-end QA that catches backend state bugs, not just UI surface issues

title: Show HN: Galois connections for composable numeric casts in Rust
why: Numeric casts are a perennial source of silent bugs (`u32::MAX as i32 == -1`), and this crate reframes them as law-checked, composable Galois connections with actual SMT proofs behind the integer families, not just tests.
notes: `Conn<A, B, K>` is a pair of monotone functions `(f, g)` with a phantom kind tag `K ∈ {L, R}`; it is `Copy`, `const`-constructible, heap-free, and the crate is `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]`. MSRV Rust 1.88, Edition 2024, MIT, and a stated port of the Haskell `connections` library. Kind discipline is structural: calling `.floor()` on an L-kind Conn (or `.ceil()` on R) is a compile error.

Every connection ships a proptest battery of nine laws (both Galois laws, unit/counit closure and kernel laws, monotonicity both directions, and `inner∘ceil` idempotency); a tenth law, `conn_floor_le_ceil`, is asserted only on two-sided `ConnK` triples whose `inner` is an injective embedding. Float comparisons use an `N5` lattice where NaN is reflexive and sits between ±∞.

Novelty claim: the generated fixed-width integer, Q-format, `NonZero`, and iso families carry Kani/CBMC harnesses proving the Galois-law predicates over the full bit-width domain, not just sampled inputs. Pointer-width `usize`/`isize` are the exception (CBMC models one concrete width per run) and fall back to host-target proptest.

Float SMT coverage is deliberately narrower: the `f64→f32` ULP-walk (`ceil_f64_f32`/`floor_f64_f32`) is proven to converge in ≤2 iterations for every finite non-NaN f64 via three tiered harnesses (`t0_` full domain, `t1_` |x|≤1e6, `t2_` the [1,2) binade). These do not prove full float Galois laws over NaN/±∞ nor cover float→integer/fixed helper bodies; those rely on proptest. Full proof tree runs in under two minutes.

The `compose!` macro folds a chain of pairwise Conns into one `Conn<Src, Dst>` at compile time, and the composite obeys the same laws by construction; applying any destructor (`upper`, `lower`, `ceil`, `floor`) exits the Conn algebra and forfeits the static guarantees of the full chain.

Domain coverage (largely feature-gated) spans Q-format fixed-point over i8/u8…i128/u128 backings, std::net IPv4/IPv6 addresses, char codepoints (surrogate-gap-aware), sortable big/little-endian byte encodings, and `time`/`hifitime`/`uhlc` clock and duration bridges. The `f16` and `try_trait` features require nightly.
summary: `connections` is a Rust crate (Show HN, ~14 points) that models numeric and ordered-type conversions as first-class Galois connections, giving casts explicit, law-checked rounding and saturation semantics instead of the silent behavior of `as`/`From`/`Into`. Each `Conn<A, B, K>` is a Copy, const-constructible, unsafe-free pair of monotone maps tagged L or R, with kind discipline enforced at compile time (e.g. `.floor()` on an L-kind Conn fails to compile), and a `compose!` macro that statically folds conversion chains into a single law-preserving Conn. Every connection is checked against nine proptest laws (both Galois laws, closure/kernel/monotonicity, idempotency), with a tenth sandwich law on injective two-sided triples; float ordering uses an N5 lattice that handles NaN and ±∞. Its headline claim is formal verification: the fixed-width integer, Q-format, NonZero, and iso families carry Kani/CBMC harnesses proving Galois laws over the full bit-width domain, while float coverage is narrower and limited to proving the `f64→f32` ULP-walk converges in ≤2 iterations for finite non-NaN inputs. Pointer-width `usize`/`isize` casts and float→integer/fixed helper bodies fall back to proptest rather than SMT. The crate is MIT-licensed, targets MSRV 1.88 / Edition 2024, is a port of the Haskell `connections` library, and gates domains like fixed-point, time/hifitime/uhlc, and f16/try_trait behind cargo features.
novelty: Kani SMT harnesses providing full bit-width formal proofs of Galois laws on integer/Q-format cast families; composable adjoint-triple type in stable Rust with compile-time kind discipline
audience: Rust systems/embedded engineers who need provably correct numeric type conversions and composable cast chains

title: Tower Semiconductor revives shuttered Panasonic-era fab in $3 billion Japan photonics expansion — METI-backed plan targets $3.6 billion revenue by 2028
why: The AI buildout's shift from copper to optical interconnects is now visibly reshaping foundry capital plans, and Tower's disclosures give hard numbers on how much silicon photonics demand is already contracted through 2027.
notes: Tower committed up to $3 billion (net of grants), backed by Japan's METI, in a two-track 300mm expansion; only Track One (reviving the shuttered Panasonic-era Arai fab as Fab 6 for silicon photonics plus expanding Fab 7 in Uozu, production-ready Q4 2027) underpins the new targets. Track Two (a new fab adjacent to Fab 7, 'highly accretive beginning 2029') has no signed agreements.

Revised 2028 model: ~$3.6B revenue and ~$1.2B net profit, implying ~33% net margin vs ~14% today; that is +29% revenue and +60% profit over the prior 2028 model ($2.8B / $750M) reaffirmed in May. 2025 actuals were $1.566B revenue and $220M net profit.

Silicon photonics revenue reached $228M in 2025 (from $106M in 2024) and a $380M annualized Q4 run rate (which Ellwanger said includes some non-wafer engineering revenue). Tower disclosed $1.3B in contracted 2027 silicon photonics revenue from its largest customers, backed by $290M in prepayments already collected.

Customers cited: Innolight (400G/800G/1.6T transceivers on the PH18 platform) and Marvell, which said in June it shipped more than 5 million coherent photonic ICs made with Tower; Tower claims 50+ active silicon photonics customers and 200 Gb/s-per-lane capacity for 1.6T devices.

Competitive/legal context: GlobalFoundries paid $453M for Singapore's Advanced Micro Foundry in November 2025 and is pursuing patent-infringement claims against Tower; TSMC's COUPE co-packaged optics tracks Nvidia's roadmap. MarketsandMarkets pegs the silicon photonics market at $2.65B in 2025 growing to $9.65B by 2030 (29.5% CAGR).

Caveats Tower flags: construction delays, equipment lead times, permitting, and METI grant covenants that 'may result in loss of a portion or all of the grant funds'; margin expansion assumes sustained AI/data-center optics demand from a concentrated group of very large customers. Tower reached this after Intel's $5.4B acquisition collapsed in August 2023 (Chinese regulators; $353M termination fee).
summary: Tower Semiconductor announced a two-track expansion of its 300mm silicon photonics, silicon germanium, and advanced packaging operations in Japan, committing up to $3 billion net of grants with METI backing. The centerpiece of Track One is reviving the shuttered Panasonic-era Arai fab (idle since July 2022) as Fab 6 for silicon photonics and advanced optical packaging, with production readiness expected in Q4 2027, plus expanded output at Fab 7 in Uozu; CEO Russell Ellwanger argues reusing a dormant building next to a qualified, cash-generating fab lets Tower reach readiness roughly 18 months ahead of greenfield builds. Tower raised its 2028 model to about $3.6B revenue and $1.2B net profit (~33% margin vs ~14% today), up 29% and 60% respectively from prior targets, resting entirely on Track One. It cited silicon photonics revenue of $228M in 2025 (from $106M in 2024), a $380M Q4 run rate, and $1.3B in contracted 2027 revenue with $290M in prepayments collected, plus customers including Innolight and Marvell (5M+ coherent photonic ICs shipped). Tower flags construction, permitting, equipment, and METI grant-covenant risks, along with dependence on concentrated AI/data-center optics demand. It competes most directly with GlobalFoundries, which bought Advanced Micro Foundry for $453M in November 2025 and is litigating patent claims against Tower.
novelty: Specific disclosure of $1.3B contracted 2027 silicon photonics revenue with $290M prepayments collected, plus revised 2028 targets ($3.6B revenue / $1.2B net profit implying ~33% margin), and confirmation of shuttered Arai fab revival as Fab 6 for silicon photonics
audience: Hardware engineers and infrastructure architects tracking AI datacenter optical interconnect supply chain

title: Linus Torvalds rebukes anti-AI stances in the Linux kernel code review process, says 'Linux is not one of those anti-AI projects' — creator embraces AI as just a tool and 'clearly a useful one'
why: The maintainer of the most consequential open-source project just publicly reversed his 2024 AI skepticism and told anti-AI kernel developers to fork or walk away, signaling that LLM-assisted code review is becoming a legitimate part of upstream workflows.
notes: On the LKML, Torvalds wrote 'Linux is not one of those anti-AI projects,' told dissenters they can 'fork it. Or just walk away,' and called AI 'a tool, just like other tools we use... and it's clearly a useful one,' a reversal from his 2024 dismissal of AI as overhyped.
The dispute centered on Sashiko, an opt-in (per-mailing-list), multi-stage AI code review tool that only comments on kernel patches and takes no autonomous action.

Sashiko's project page claims a 53.6% bug detection rate on patches that had already passed human review, arguing this beats human-level review; its false positive rate is self-described as 'within 20%' and admitted to be harder to measure.

Google engineer Roman Gushchin is one of Sashiko's creators; developer Laurent Pinchart proposed triaging its output before sending comments to authors, citing Software Freedom Conservancy guidelines on AI-generated code, which Gushchin and Torvalds framed as anti-LLM.

Torvalds said the tool 'keeps finding embarrassing bugs,' vowed to 'very loudly ignore' people arguing against others using it, and noted 'it's not like natural intelligence is always all that great either.'
The piece frames this against open-source projects like Gentoo, Curl, and Ghostty that have limited or banned LLM-generated contributions over 'slop' concerns.
summary: Tom's Hardware reports that Linus Torvalds used a long Linux kernel mailing list comment to defend AI-assisted tooling, stating 'Linux is not one of those anti-AI projects' and telling objectors they can fork the project or walk away. The trigger was Sashiko, an opt-in, multi-stage AI code review tool co-created by Google engineer Roman Gushchin that comments on kernel patches without taking autonomous action; its project page claims a 53.6% bug detection rate on already human-reviewed patches and a self-described false positive rate 'within 20%.' Developer Laurent Pinchart proposed triaging Sashiko's output before sending comments, citing Software Freedom Conservancy AI guidelines, but Gushchin argued this would undermine the tool and Torvalds echoed that the position was anti-LLM. Torvalds called AI 'a tool... and it's clearly a useful one,' a notable reversal from his 2024 dismissal of AI as overhyped, and said the tool 'keeps finding embarrassing bugs.' The article situates the stance against other open-source projects such as Gentoo, Curl, and Ghostty that have restricted LLM-generated contributions. All performance figures come from Sashiko's own project page and are not independently verified.
novelty: Torvalds' explicit public reversal from 2024 AI skepticism to active defense of AI tooling in kernel patch review, with specific endorsement of Sashiko and a direct rebuke of anti-AI maintainers on LKML
audience: Linux kernel contributors and open-source maintainers evaluating AI-assisted code review policies

title: Tracing Agentic Failure from the Flow of Success
why: If you run multi-step agents, this proposes a way to localize which step broke a failed run without ever labeling failures, using only a handful of successful traces.
notes: OAT reframes step-level failure attribution as one-class (anomaly-detection) learning: it models the dynamics of successful agent trajectories in latent space using neural controlled differential equations (NCDEs).

Training uses only successful trajectories, no failure data and no step-level error annotations; at inference each step in a failed trajectory gets an anomaly score based on deviation from the learned success dynamics, and high-scoring steps form the predicted error set.
The authors report training on as few as 100 successful trajectories, addressing the stated bottleneck that step-level failure annotations are costly to collect and hard to scale.
Claimed efficiency: 200-5000x faster at inference than prompting-based attribution baselines, which they characterize as computationally expensive.

Claimed accuracy: +20% F1 in-domain and +7% F1 out-of-distribution over prompting-based baselines; these are the authors' own numbers and the abstract does not name the specific datasets, baselines, or backbone models.
summary: This arXiv paper introduces OAT, a method for failure attribution in LLM-based agentic systems, meaning identifying which steps in a failed trajectory caused the task to fail. The authors argue existing approaches are impractical: prompting-based pipelines are computationally expensive, and post-training approaches require step-level error annotations on failure trajectories that are costly and hard to scale. OAT instead does unsupervised attribution, training exclusively on successful trajectories via one-class learning with neural controlled differential equations to model the latent dynamics of success, then scoring each step of a failure trajectory by how much it deviates from those dynamics. Trained on only 100 successful trajectories, the authors report OAT is 200-5000x faster than prompting-based baselines while outperforming them by +20% F1 in-domain and +7% F1 out-of-distribution. The abstract presents these as the authors' results and does not specify the datasets, baselines, or agent backbones used.
novelty: Unsupervised failure attribution for LLM agents trained exclusively on successful trajectories via one-class learning with neural controlled differential equations — no step-level error annotations on failure data required.
audience: ML engineers and researchers building, evaluating, or debugging LLM-based agentic pipelines.

title: From Noisy Traces to Root Causes: Structural Trajectory Analysis and Causal Extraction for Agent Optimization
why: If you build agents optimized by an LLM-as-optimizer loop, the quality of the traces you feed it may matter more than the optimizer itself, and this paper argues naive context reduction actively corrupts the signal.
notes: STRACE (Structural TRajectory Analysis and Causal Extraction) works at two levels: batch-level failure-pattern mining to filter redundant/heterogeneous traces and keep representative failures, and within-trace causal localization over a textual dependency graph to strip non-causal steps and pinpoint the root-cause module.

The paper's core critique: large trace collections cause optimizers to overfit to low-value failures, while naive per-trace reduction (truncation, sliding windows) discards causally important evidence and yields misleading optimization signals.
Headline result: on VeruSAGE-Bench, a formal-verification task, STRACE lifts success rate from 42.5% to 58.5% (claimed 1.4x) applied to human-expert-designed agents.
The setup targets the reflection-based, LLM-as-optimizer paradigm where a frontier model diagnoses agent failures and rewrites agent policies from execution traces.
Claims to significantly outperform standard context-filtering baselines; specific baselines, ablations, and comparison methodology are not detailed in the abstract. Code is released.
summary: STRACE is a framework for building high signal-to-noise optimization contexts used to improve long-horizon LLM agents via reflection-based, LLM-as-optimizer loops. It operates at two levels: batch-level failure-pattern mining that filters redundant and heterogeneous traces to retain representative failures, and within-trace causal localization over a textual dependency graph that removes non-causal steps and identifies the true root-cause module. The authors argue this beats naive context reduction (truncation, sliding windows), which they say discards causally important evidence and produces misleading optimization signals. Empirically, STRACE is reported to outperform standard context-filtering baselines and, on the formal-verification benchmark VeruSAGE-Bench, to raise success rate from 42.5% to 58.5% (a claimed 1.4x improvement) on human-expert-designed agents. Code is publicly available. The abstract does not specify the baseline set, benchmark composition beyond the one task, or ablation detail.
novelty: Textual dependency graph for causal localization within individual agent traces, plus batch-level failure-pattern mining to filter redundant traces — combined into a single optimization context pipeline (STRACE)
audience: ML engineers building or debugging long-horizon LLM agents and reflection-based optimization loops

title: Adaptive Filtering of the KV Cache: Diagnosing and Correcting Structural-Role Bias in LLM Inference
why: If you run long-context inference over JSON or other schema-dense inputs, popular attention-based KV cache eviction can silently retain delimiters and drop the tokens holding your answers, tanking accuracy at aggressive budgets.
notes: Diagnosis: on schema-dense streams like nested JSON, non-content sink tokens (delimiters, whitespace) accumulate roughly 10x more attention energy than any content role, so H2O-style eviction (which keeps the heaviest attention mass) preferentially retains structural noise.

Structural KEY tokens are over-retained at about 1.8x the rate of answer-carrying VALUE tokens; the paper frames attention mass as signal energy and cache retention as a non-stationary filter that degrades signal-to-noise.
Failure mode is severe: exact-match accuracy collapses from 88% to 0% at a 5% cache budget. A counterfactual shows that suppressing KEY tokens is the best deployable filter.
Proposed fix is retraining-free: role-conditional allocation layered on SnapKV's windowed score, governed by a single tuned hyperparameter, closing 63-98% of the H2O gap at sub-20% budgets.
Role labels come from a 15 MB linear probe at negligible inference cost; authors caution that matching parser-level downstream accuracy remains open.

Authors are explicit about limits: the higher-budget denoising effect that matches or exceeds full-cache accuracy is small and seed-sensitive, borderline significant at B=0.50 and indistinguishable from zero at B=0.30 over four seeds.
summary: This paper argues that attention-mass-based KV cache eviction (H2O and descendants) systematically fails on schema-dense inputs such as nested JSON, because delimiter and whitespace tokens accumulate roughly an order of magnitude more accumulated attention energy than content tokens. Treated as a filter ranking tokens by "signal energy," eviction over-retains structural KEY tokens at about 1.8x the rate of answer-carrying VALUE tokens, collapsing exact-match accuracy from 88% to 0% at a 5% budget. A counterfactual experiment establishes that suppressing KEY tokens is the best deployable filter. The authors propose a retraining-free, role-conditional allocation over SnapKV's windowed score, tuned with a single hyperparameter, which closes 63-98% of the H2O gap at sub-20% budgets and, at higher budgets, modestly matches or exceeds full-cache accuracy. Token-role labels are supplied by a 15 MB linear probe at negligible inference cost. The authors are candid that the higher-budget denoising gain is small and seed-sensitive (borderline significant at B=0.50, not distinguishable from zero at B=0.30 over four seeds) and that matching parser-level downstream accuracy remains open.
novelty: Diagnosis that delimiter/whitespace tokens accumulate ~10x more attention energy than content on schema-dense inputs (nested JSON), causing KV cache eviction methods (H2O family) to systematically over-retain structural noise and drop answer-relevant tokens — and a retraining-free role-conditional fix.
audience: ML engineers optimizing long-context LLM inference with KV cache compression techniques

title: From Controlled to the Wild: Evaluation of Pentesting Agents for the Real-World
why: If you build or buy AI pentesting agents, this paper argues today's CTF/RCE benchmarks tell you almost nothing about real-world performance and proposes a scoring protocol built around validated vulnerability discovery instead.
notes: The authors argue existing pentesting-agent benchmarks optimize narrow, predefined goals (capture-the-flag, remote code execution, exploit reproduction, trajectory similarity) in simplified settings that fail to capture open-ended exploration and strategic decision-making.
Core shift: scoring moves from task completion to validated vulnerability discovery on targets that span multiple attack surfaces and vulnerability classes.

Mechanism combines structured ground truth with LLM-based semantic matching to map agent findings to known vulnerabilities, plus a bipartite resolution algorithm to score under ambiguity (findings and ground-truth entries need not map one-to-one).

Protocol adds continuous ground-truth maintenance, repeated/cumulative evaluation to account for stochastic agents, efficiency metrics, and a reduced-suite selection mechanism for sustainable experimentation.

Authors release expert-annotated ground truth and evaluation code for reproducibility; the paper is a protocol proposal and does not, in the abstract, report comparative results across specific agents.
Positioned as an arXiv preprint (cs.AI/cs.CR); claims of improved realism are the authors' and are not independently benchmarked in the provided text.
summary: The paper proposes an evaluation protocol for AI pentesting agents that replaces task-completion benchmarks (CTF, remote code execution, exploit reproduction, trajectory similarity) with validated vulnerability discovery on complex targets spanning multiple attack surfaces and vulnerability classes. It combines structured ground truth with LLM-based semantic matching to identify discovered vulnerabilities, and uses a bipartite resolution step to score findings under realistic ambiguity where one finding may map to several ground-truth entries and vice versa. The protocol further specifies continuous ground-truth maintenance, repeated and cumulative evaluation to handle stochastic agent behavior, efficiency metrics, and a reduced-suite selection mechanism for sustainable repeated runs. The authors release expert-annotated ground truth and code to support reproducibility. The abstract frames this as extending the state of the art toward operationally informative agent comparison, but does not include head-to-head agent results, leaving empirical validation to the full paper.
novelty: Evaluation protocol that replaces CTF/RCE/trajectory-similarity benchmarks with validated vulnerability discovery across multi-surface real-world targets, using bipartite resolution scoring and LLM-based semantic matching to handle ambiguity.
audience: Security researchers and AI agent engineers building or evaluating offensive security tooling

title: How Our Rust-to-Zig Rewrite Is Going
why: A rare longitudinal retrospective on a real 300K-line Rust-to-Zig compiler rewrite, with concrete build-time benchmarks and memory-safety bug counts, gives you evidence instead of forum tribalism when weighing the two languages for systems work.
notes: The rewrite of Roc's compiler took 487 days to reach feature parity; the codebase grew from ~354K Rust LoC to ~464K Zig LoC as features were added alongside the port, so it is explicitly not an apples-to-apples comparison. The author contrasts this with Bun's 11-day Zig-to-Rust port, noting theirs was a direct port while Roc's was a rewrite motivated by architectural changes.

Build times: Zig 0.17 with `zig build --watch -fincremental` rebuilds a change in ~35ms vs. Rust 1.97's 3.4s incremental (down from Rust 1.85's 10.0s over the same 18 months). Caveat: `-fincremental` is broken by a bug in stable Zig 0.16.0 and only works on x86-64, so Roc has not yet reaped this in practice; getting the fix requires nightly 0.17.0 with breaking changes.

Memory-corruption bug counts, classified by Claude Opus 4.8: 21 of 2,596 total reports in the Rust era, 10 of 431 in the Zig era. None of the 21 Rust cases were in compiler logic (all miscompilations from emitted code); 8 of 10 Zig cases were miscompilations, and the 2 remaining were use-after-frees in error-reporting paths (roc check, roc bundle) that the borrow checker would have caught. Author's takeaway: choosing Rust, Zig ReleaseFast, or Zig ReleaseSafe would have made no appreciable difference.

Rationale for Zig: the original Rust compiler already had ~1,200 `unsafe` uses because emitting machine code is inherently memory-unsafe work, blunting Rust's safe-subset advantage. Other cited factors: faster builds, Zig's ecosystem-wide assumption of granular/arena allocators and struct-of-arrays (vs. Rust's global-allocator assumption), and more relevant off-the-shelf Zig code for niche needs like emitting LLVM bitcode.

Zero-parse deserialization: all compiler data structures use arrays with 32-bit indices in struct-of-arrays form, so on-disk cache can be written as-is and reloaded via pointer relocations with no parsing step (a technique borrowed from Zig's compiler and game programming).

New-compiler wins for Roc programmers: `roc build --opt=size` produces a 31KB wasm binary for the sample game vs. >62KB originally; string interpolation inside pattern matching enables type-safe HTTP routing with zero heap allocations; automatic hot code loading and deterministic cross-compilation (e.g. `--target=x64musl`). A formal 0.1.0 release is targeted for later in the year.
summary: Richard Feldman reports on the Roc team's 487-day rewrite of their ~300K-line compiler from Rust to Zig, which recently reached feature parity (formal 0.1.0 targeted later this year). The piece frames the choice as workload-specific rather than a verdict on either language: because a compiler emitting machine code is inherently memory-unsafe (the Rust version already carried ~1,200 `unsafe` uses), Zig was chosen for faster builds, granular/arena allocator and struct-of-arrays support, and better tooling for unavoidably unsafe code. On memory safety, bug counts classified by Claude Opus 4.8 show 21/2,596 corruption bugs in the Rust era vs. 10/431 in the Zig era, with only 2 Zig cases (UAFs in error reporting) that Rust's borrow checker would have caught; the author concludes the tooling choice made no appreciable practical difference. On build times, Zig 0.17's `-fincremental` hits ~35ms rebuilds vs. Rust 1.97's 3.4s, though that feature is broken in stable 0.16 and x86-64 only, so the gain is not yet realized. The rewrite also unlocked user-facing features: a 31KB (vs. >62KB) wasm game binary, hot code loading, deterministic cross-compilation, zero-parse cache deserialization, and zero-allocation HTTP routing via string interpolation in pattern matching. The author repeatedly stresses the comparison is not apples-to-apples given ~50% codebase growth and many concurrent design changes.
novelty: First longitudinal retrospective of a real 300K+ LoC Rust→Zig compiler rewrite with concrete build-time benchmarks and memory-safety bug-count data across both eras.
audience: Systems programmers and compiler engineers evaluating Rust vs. Zig for long-lived, memory-intensive codebases.

title: Google ordered to open Android and Search to rivals in Europe
why: If you build AI assistants or search-adjacent products, the EU just mandated that Android grant third-party assistants Gemini-level system access, opening a distribution channel on the world's largest mobile OS that was previously closed.
notes: Two DMA decisions issued Thursday: one on Android AI-assistant interoperability, one on Google Search data sharing. Deadlines: begin sharing Search data by January 2027, implement Android changes by July 2027.

Android ruling requires rival AI assistants (the article names ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity) to get 'comparable' system access to Gemini: responding to 'Hey Google' voice commands, interacting with apps, and fuller device hardware access, with users (not Google) deciding what data and hardware a tool can reach.

Search decision forces Google to share Search-generated data with competing search engines and, notably, AI chatbots, which the EU treats as functionally equivalent to search engines in some cases; the remedy broadly echoes the US search antitrust case.

Enforcement lever is non-financial: these are compliance procedures, but non-compliance can trigger fines up to 10% of Google's annual worldwide turnover (potentially tens of billions). EU says it will cap how shared search data can be used and let Google vet which services get deeper Android access for safety.

Google's Kent Walker called the rulings a risk to 'vital privacy and security guardrails for millions of Europeans' and said they 'discount extensive evidence of user harm'; the article presents no independent evaluation of that claim.
Read as a signal for Apple, which declined to ship Siri AI in Europe citing DMA interoperability as a safety risk; the rulings hint at how Brussels may approach other gatekeepers.
summary: The European Commission issued two Digital Markets Act compliance decisions against Google, ordering it to open Android and Google Search to rivals. The Android decision requires Google to give competing AI assistants system-level access comparable to Gemini's, including responding to 'Hey Google,' interacting with apps, and using device hardware, with the user rather than Google controlling access; the article cites ChatGPT, Claude, and Perplexity as potential beneficiaries. The Search decision compels Google to share certain Search data with rival search engines and with AI chatbots, which the EU classifies as functionally equivalent to search engines, a remedy the piece says echoes the US search antitrust case. Google must begin sharing Search data by January 2027 and implement Android changes by July 2027; non-compliance could draw fines up to 10% of annual worldwide turnover. Google's Kent Walker argued the rulings undermine privacy and security for European users, while the Commission framed them as promoting competition and choice. The Verge notes the decisions may preview how Brussels handles other gatekeepers, including Apple, which cited the DMA when withholding Siri AI in Europe.
novelty: Specific DMA compliance deadlines and technical interoperability mandates for third-party AI assistants on Android at system-capability parity with Gemini
audience: AI product engineers and founders building assistants or search alternatives targeting the EU market

title: SingGuard-NSFA: Extensible Guardrails for Agentic AI via Generative Reasoning and Real-Time Classification
why: If you are deploying agents in production, this paper offers an open-weight guardrail stack that claims both interpretable offline auditing and ~50ms real-time threat detection from one backbone, plus a taxonomy and multilingual benchmark you can test against.
notes: The NSFA taxonomy organizes 185 risk variants into a CIA-triad-grounded hierarchy (confidentiality, integrity, availability), cross-validated against three OWASP guideline sets; targeted threats include prompt injection, sensitive information extraction, malicious code requests, dangerous tool misuse, and resource exhaustion.

Benchmark suite spans 133 languages: over 93K purpose-built samples covering both user queries and agent responses, plus 3,435 cross-source samples adapted from five existing public agent-security datasets.

Dual-mode architecture: an SFT-based generative reasoning path for interpretable offline auditing, and discriminative classification heads on a frozen backbone for real-time detection at roughly 50ms.
Four open models released at 0.8B, 2B, 4B, and 9B parameters; all report >=94% F1 on the purpose-built benchmarks, claimed to beat the strongest competing guardrails by 6 to 12 absolute F1 points.

The 9B model reaches 91.29% F1 on cross-source evaluation with a more balanced precision-recall trade-off; ablations claim the classification heads can extend detection scope beyond original training, positioning the method as a plug-in enhancement.

Caveat: these are authors' self-reported numbers from an arXiv preprint; competing-guardrail identities, evaluation methodology details, and independent replication are not established in the abstract.
summary: SingGuard-NSFA (nsfaguard) is a guardrail framework aimed at securing agentic AI systems against operational threats such as prompt injection, sensitive information extraction, malicious code requests, dangerous tool misuse, and resource exhaustion. The authors introduce the NSFA taxonomy, which maps 185 risk variants onto a CIA-triad-grounded hierarchy cross-validated against three OWASP guideline sets, and build a benchmark suite spanning 133 languages with over 93K purpose-built samples plus 3,435 cross-source samples from five public agent-security datasets. The system uses a dual-mode design: SFT-based generative reasoning for interpretable offline auditing and discriminative classification heads on a frozen backbone for real-time detection at about 50ms. Four open models (0.8B, 2B, 4B, 9B) are released; all report at least 94% F1 on purpose-built benchmarks, said to exceed the strongest competing guardrails by 6 to 12 absolute points, with the 9B model reaching 91.29% F1 on cross-source evaluation. Ablations claim the classification heads can extend a guardrail's detection scope beyond its original training scope, supporting a plug-in enhancement story. All figures are self-reported in this preprint and await independent verification.
novelty: NSFA risk taxonomy (185 variants, CIA-triad-grounded) combined with dual-mode generative+discriminative guardrail architecture enabling both interpretable auditing and ~50ms real-time detection from a single frozen backbone
audience: ML engineers and security researchers building or deploying agentic AI pipelines who need production-ready guardrails against prompt injection, tool misuse, and data exfiltration

title: ShortOPD: Recovering Pruned LLMs with Short-to-Long On-Policy Distillation
why: If you ship pruned LLMs, this paper shows why they collapse on free-form generation and offers a recovery recipe that cuts training cost ~4x by truncating the repetitive rollouts that were burning your budget.
notes: Diagnosis: after structured pruning, greedy pass@1 nearly vanishes but pass@k recovers substantially under repeated sampling, which the authors read as useful generations being demoted rather than erased; the dominant failure mode in this recoverable regime is suffix repetition.
Method: ShortOPD uses On-Policy Distillation, training on the compressed model's own rollout states with dense token-level supervision from the pre-compression model as a frozen teacher.

Key trick: a short-to-long schedule detects teacher-confirmed repetitive suffixes, treats the surviving prefix as each rollout's effective length, and allocates future rollout budget to lengths the policy can currently use, avoiding wasted early budget on low-information repetitive tails.
Claimed results across math, code, and open-ended generation: ~9x the unrecovered compressed score and 1.6-4.4x over standard recipes (SFT without KD, KD, and SeqKD).
Efficiency claim: matches a fixed 8192-token rollout horizon within two points using 8.5 vs 35.9 hours of training (about a quarter of the time) and 71% fewer rollout tokens.

Caveats from the text: results are self-reported on an arXiv preprint with no baseline methodology, model family, or pruning ratios disclosed in the abstract; the framing targets moving pruning evaluation beyond perplexity and multiple-choice benchmarks.
summary: ShortOPD is an arXiv preprint addressing a gap in structured pruning of LLMs: compressed checkpoints validate well on multiple-choice recognition but collapse on the free-form generation that deployment requires. The authors observe that greedy pass@1 nearly vanishes after pruning while pass@k recovers under repeated sampling, arguing useful generations are demoted rather than erased, and that the main failure mode is suffix repetition. Their recovery method uses On-Policy Distillation, training on the compressed model's own on-policy states with dense token-level supervision from the pre-compression model as a frozen teacher. The core contribution is a short-to-long schedule that detects teacher-confirmed repetitive suffixes, treats the surviving prefix as the effective rollout length, and allocates future rollout budget accordingly, avoiding wasted compute on repetitive tails. The authors report recovering compressed models to roughly 9x their unrecovered score and 1.6-4.4x over SFT-without-KD, KD, and SeqKD baselines across math, code, and open-ended generation. They further claim it matches a fixed 8192-token rollout horizon within two points using 8.5 versus 35.9 hours of training and 71% fewer rollout tokens.
novelty: Short-to-long rollout scheduling for on-policy distillation that dynamically truncates repetitive suffixes — cutting training cost 4x while matching full-length recovery quality
audience: ML engineers deploying compressed/pruned LLMs in production where free-form generation quality matters

title: Boogu-Image-0.1: Boosting Open-Source Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation
why: A permissively licensed unified understanding-plus-generation model family that claims near-closed-source image quality for a roughly $400K base-model training budget is a concrete data point on how cheaply the open ecosystem can now approach the frontier.
notes: Boogu-Image-0.1 ships four variants: Base, Turbo, Edit, and Edit-Turbo, covering text-to-image generation, fast inference, and instruction-based editing.
The authors report training on only 208.62 million unique images, unusually small for a competitive generator, with a stated theoretical base-model training cost of roughly $400K.

The claimed gains come from targeted work on model understanding, data quality, and training pipelines, plus agentic inference-time scaling to lift generation and editing quality under constrained compute.
Supports bilingual Chinese-English text rendering inside generated images; weights, code, and recipes are released under Apache 2.0.
The paper contrasts itself with closed systems it calls Nano-Banana-Pro and GPT-Image-2, arguing those rely on undisclosed system-level integration rather than a single model.

Caveats: performance claims are self-reported ('matches or surpasses' open-source, 'approaching' closed-source) with no benchmark numbers or methodology in the abstract, and the $400K cost is described as theoretical.
summary: Boogu-Image-0.1 is an open-source unified multimodal understanding and generation model family (Base, Turbo, Edit, and Edit-Turbo) released under Apache 2.0 with weights, code, and training recipes. The authors claim competitive high-quality text-to-image generation, fast inference, instruction-based editing, and bilingual Chinese-English text rendering, achieved by combining improvements in model understanding, data quality, and training pipelines with agentic inference-time scaling under constrained compute. They report training on only 208.62 million unique images and a theoretical base-model training cost of about $400K. The paper positions itself against closed systems it names Nano-Banana-Pro and GPT-Image-2, arguing those achieve strong results through undisclosed system-level integration rather than a single model. Comprehensive evaluations are said to show Boogu-Image-0.1 matching or surpassing other open-source models and approaching leading closed-source systems, though the abstract provides no benchmark figures or methodology to substantiate these comparisons.
novelty: Open-source unified image understanding + generation model family achieving near-closed-source benchmark parity at ~$400K training cost via agentic inference-time scaling
audience: ML engineers and researchers building or evaluating multimodal generation pipelines

title: Harness Handbook: Making Evolving Agent Harnesses Readable,Navigable, and Editable
why: If you maintain an agent harness, the paper argues your real bottleneck is not generating edits but finding where behavior lives in the code, and it proposes a reusable index to close that gap.
notes: Frames a specific problem: modification requests describe desired behavior, but repos are organized by files/modules, so mapping behavior to code locations stays a manual bottleneck even with code search, repo indexing, and long-context inspection.

Harness Handbook is a behavior-centric representation synthesized automatically from the harness codebase via static analysis plus LLM-assisted structuring, with each behavior linked to its source locations.

Behavior-Guided Progressive Disclosure (BGPD) navigates agents from high-level behaviors down to implementation details and verifies candidate edit locations against the current source (a check against stale mappings).

Evaluated on diverse modification requests from two open-source harnesses; Handbook-assisted planning is reported to improve behavior localization and edit-plan quality while using fewer planner tokens versus baseline.
Authors claim the largest gains on the hardest cases for naive search: scattered edit sites, rarely executed code paths, and cross-module interactions.
Caveat: the abstract reports no absolute metrics, baseline definitions, harness names, or token counts; magnitude of improvement and generality beyond two repos are unstated in the provided text.
summary: The Harness Handbook paper targets the maintenance problem of agent harnesses, the code that builds prompts, manages state, invokes tools, and coordinates execution around a foundation model. It argues that as models, APIs, and requirements change, the main obstacle to editing a harness is behavior localization: finding every code location that implements a requested behavior, which is hard because production harnesses are large, tightly coupled, and behaviorally distributed while repos are organized by files and modules. The authors introduce the Harness Handbook, a behavior-centric index synthesized automatically from source via static analysis and LLM-assisted structuring that links behaviors to their code, plus Behavior-Guided Progressive Disclosure (BGPD), a protocol that walks from high-level behaviors to implementation details and verifies candidate locations against the current source. On modification requests from two open-source harnesses, they report that Handbook-assisted planning improves localization and edit-plan quality while using fewer planner tokens, with the biggest gains on scattered sites, rarely executed paths, and cross-module interactions. The abstract states these results qualitatively but does not include specific metrics, baselines, or the harness names in the provided text.
novelty: Behavior-centric codebase representation (Harness Handbook) + BGPD navigation protocol specifically for localizing edit sites within agent harnesses — distinct from general code search or RAG-over-repo approaches
audience: Engineers building or maintaining production AI agent systems who must evolve complex harness codebases over time

title: Self-Improvements in Modern Agentic Systems: A Survey
why: Self-improving agents are moving from research demos toward deployment, and this survey offers a single vocabulary for reasoning about how they actually change over time.
notes: Models a modern agent as a configuration that couples a foundation model with an operational scaffold: prompts, memory, tools, and control logic.
Formalizes self-improvement as a 'self-induced update operator' that obtains and commits updates to one of two targets: model parameters OR scaffold components.
Organizes prior work along two axes: what gets updated (the target) and the signals that drive the change.
Stated framing goal is 'controllable evolution, or adaptation, from experience with minimal or even no human input.'
Beyond the taxonomy, it also reviews applications, evaluation methodology, and open problems; authors maintain it as a living document with technical updates tracked at a companion URL.
This is a survey/framework paper: it synthesizes and organizes existing work rather than presenting new experimental results or benchmarks.
summary: This arXiv survey proposes a system-level framework for understanding self-improving agentic systems, which the authors describe as adaptive systems that convert experience into accumulated capability gains. It models a modern agent as a foundation model coupled with an operational scaffold (prompts, memory, tools, and control logic), and formalizes self-improvement as a 'self-induced update operator' that commits updates either to model parameters or to scaffold components. Prior work is organized along two axes: the update target and the signals that drive change. The paper's stated goal is characterizing controllable evolution from experience with minimal or no human input. It additionally reviews applications, evaluation, and open problems, and is maintained as a living document at a companion URL. As a survey it offers unification and taxonomy rather than new empirical results.
novelty: Formal taxonomy of agent self-improvement as a "self-induced update operator" acting on either model parameters or scaffold components (prompts, memory, tools, control logic) — a system-level unification of prior fragmented work
audience: ML researchers and senior engineers designing or evaluating self-improving agentic pipelines

title: Function-Aware Fill-in-the-Middle as Mid-Training for Coding Agent Foundation Models
why: If you build coding agents, this paper argues you can bake the tool-call-and-continue reasoning pattern into a base model with cheap self-supervised code masking, before any expensive agentic RL, and get consistent SWE-Bench gains.
notes: Core claim: a coding agent's action-observation-continuation loop is structurally isomorphic to a function call site (caller binds args, callee returns a value computed elsewhere, downstream code consumes it), so ordinary code at internet scale already contains this conditioning structure.

Method: function-aware fill-in-the-middle (FIM) mid-training that masks whole functions selected via program dependency graph analysis plus a 'complexity-inferability double criterion,' rather than random spans.
Corpus: 2.6B decontaminated tokens from 968 GitHub repositories, Python only; applied to Qwen2.5-Coder-Instruct 7B/14B and Qwen3-8B, followed by existing agentic post-training.
Reported gains: SWE-Bench-Verified +2.8/+3.0 (7B/14B) and +3.2 (Qwen3-8B); SWE-Bench-Lite +3.7/+4.0/+5.4. Improvements hold across three post-training pipelines (R2E-Gym, SWE-Smith, SWE-Lego).

Secondary claim: mid-training mitigates the capability erosion that agentic post-training otherwise causes on non-agent coding (LiveCodeBench) and non-coding tool-use (tau-bench, BFCL), with the function-call inductive bias transferring despite a Python-only corpus.
Caveats to flag: abstract only, no ablation or baseline detail here, single model family lineage (Qwen), and single-digit absolute gains without variance reported.
summary: This arXiv paper proposes function-aware fill-in-the-middle (FIM) as a mid-training stage for coding agent foundation models, sitting between pretraining and agentic post-training. The authors argue that an agent's action-observation-continuation loop is structurally isomorphic to a function call site, so masking and reconstructing functions in ordinary code provides self-supervised signal for integrating external tool returns into ongoing reasoning. Functions to mask are chosen via program dependency graph analysis and a complexity-inferability double criterion, not at random. They mid-train Qwen2.5-Coder-Instruct (7B/14B) and Qwen3-8B on a 2.6B-token decontaminated, Python-only corpus from 968 GitHub repositories, then apply existing agentic pipelines. They report SWE-Bench-Verified gains of +2.8/+3.0/+3.2 and SWE-Bench-Lite gains of +3.7/+4.0/+5.4, holding across R2E-Gym, SWE-Smith, and SWE-Lego post-training, and claim the approach also reduces post-training capability erosion on LiveCodeBench, tau-bench, and BFCL. Only the abstract is provided, so ablations, baselines, and variance are not verifiable from this text.
novelty: Structural isomorphism between agent action-observation-continuation loops and function call sites, exploited as a self-supervised FIM mid-training objective — not a known training recipe.
audience: ML engineers building or fine-tuning coding agents on open-weight models.

title: Windows 0-day drops the same day Microsoft releases record number of patches
why: A working, publicly available Windows elevation-of-privilege exploit landed the same day as a record Patch Tuesday, meaning your fleet has an unpatched 0-day primitive circulating before Microsoft can respond.
notes: HiveLegacy is an elevation-of-privilege exploit in the Windows User Profile Service; it lets a limited-rights user modify an administrator account's classes registry hive, the resource that controls which application opens a given file type in Explorer.

The article calls it a 'pretty powerful primitive': a low-privilege user can alter an admin account's registry without holding admin rights themselves, and the author notes processes (not just users) could likely leverage it with more work.
Preconditions as written: the attacker must know the credentials of one other account (admin status not required) and the username of a third account on the same machine.

Published by pseudonymous researcher NightmareEclypse, now at nine such disclosed exploits, who cites dissatisfaction with Microsoft's handling of their bug reports; multiple researchers say the exploit works.
The proof-of-concept was deliberately stripped down by the author to reduce direct weaponization, per the report.
Timing: dropped the same day Microsoft shipped what Ars Technica describes as a record number of security patches, forcing Microsoft to scramble on an unpatched flaw.
summary: Pseudonymous researcher NightmareEclypse published HiveLegacy, a working Windows elevation-of-privilege exploit, on the same day Microsoft released what Ars Technica calls a record number of security patches. The flaw resides in the Windows User Profile Service and lets a low-privilege user modify an administrator account's classes registry hive, the resource governing file-type-to-application associations in Explorer, without the attacker holding admin rights. As written, the exploit requires the attacker to know the credentials of one other (non-admin) account and the username of a third account on the machine. Multiple researchers confirm it works, and the author says the included proof-of-concept was stripped down to limit malicious use. It is the ninth such exploit from NightmareEclypse, who has publicly complained about Microsoft's handling of their bug reports, leaving Microsoft scrambling to patch an unaddressed zero-day.
novelty: Working EoP 0-day (HiveLegacy) publicly dropped same day as record Microsoft Patch Tuesday, by a serial pseudonymous researcher now at 9 disclosed exploits
audience: Windows security engineers, enterprise sysadmins, and vulnerability researchers

title: Show HN: I RL-trained an agent that trains models with RL (for –$1.3k)
why: A single hobbyist showing, for ~$1,275 in credits, that you can RL-train an agent whose actions are writing complete RL training jobs, with real GPU runs as the reward, is a concrete demonstration of recursive self-improvement plumbing you can actually read and reproduce.
notes: Two nested RL stacks: the outer loop trains a Qwen3.6-35B-A3B trainer agent (LoRA rank 8, lr 4e-5, group size 8) via Tinker's importance-sampling GRPO; the inner loop trains small Qwen3-0.6B/1.7B base models via prime-rl GRPO on Runpod. The agent's action is authoring a verifiers env, rubric, and prime-rl config; the inner model's pre/post hidden-eval uplift flows back up as the outer reward.

Episode reward is a weighted sum (author's live weights 0.35 validation efficiency / 0.60 job quality / 0.05 train speed), where job quality is 0.25*post-score + 0.75*signed uplift over the best untrained baseline. Caveat the author flags: the agent-facing prompt showed a simplified reward view, and the CSV -1.0 values are a logging sentinel, not a reward.

Reward rose from ~0.0 to a ~0.63 peak over 54 outer steps across three warm-started pilots (7, 7b, 7c), running ~1,750 real inner GPU jobs. The author decomposes learning into two rungs: first process reliability (converting failed/dead jobs into completed episodes, reward to ~0.26 with flat quality), then actual model quality (job grade 0.30 to 0.41, post-score from ~0.04 noise to a sustained 0.22-0.48).

On the held-out 'triage' task family (never trained on), mean reward rose from 0.399 (base) to 0.545 at step 34, then plateaued/dipped to 0.492 by step 54; the released Hugging Face LoRA adapter (~560MB, Apache-2.0) is the step-34 transfer-peak checkpoint.

Emergent behavior after adding a get_baseline_scores tool and uplift grading: the agent flipped from picking the weaker 0.6B model in 77/79 early episodes to using the 1.7B model in 42% then 95% of episodes, and adoption of the exposed prime-rl config surface rose from 21% to ~78%, tuning temperature, optimizer, scheduler, and loss.

Cost: headline arc ~£950 (~$1,275) at £0.745/$1, split ~£605 Runpod + ~£345 Tinker; warm-pool inner jobs ran ~$0.13-0.30 each. Honesty caveats: pilots, GPU benchmarking, and dead ends cost several hundred more, cold benchmark jobs hit ~$0.37, and agent-written code runs in a timeout-guarded subprocess on the host, not a container.
summary: A developer (Show HN post) open-sourced a pipeline with two nested reinforcement-learning loops: an outer loop RL-trains a Qwen3.6-35B-A3B trainer agent (LoRA, via Thinking Machines' Tinker and importance-sampling GRPO) whose action is to write a full inner-loop training job, a verifiers environment plus rubric plus prime-rl config, which then actually trains a small Qwen3-0.6B/1.7B model on Runpod GPUs. The inner model's measured uplift on a hidden eval becomes the outer reward, so no simulation is involved. Over 54 outer steps across three warm-started pilots and roughly 1,750 real GPU jobs, reported reward climbed from ~0.0 to a ~0.63 peak, and performance transferred to a held-out 'triage' task family (0.399 base to 0.545 at step 34, plateauing to 0.492 by step 54). The author documents emergent behavior, the agent shifting from the weaker 0.6B to the 1.7B model (42% to 95% of episodes) and adopting the prime-rl config surface (21% to ~78%) after a baseline-scores tool and uplift grading were added. Everything is released, including the step-34 LoRA adapter on Hugging Face (Apache-2.0), harness, task families, reward code, and failure retros. Total headline-arc cost was ~£950 (~$1,275); the author flags honesty caveats around reward-weight simplification in the agent prompt, extra unbudgeted pilot costs, and that agent-written code runs in a subprocess rather than a container.
novelty: Nested RL loops where the outer agent's action space is writing complete RL training jobs (verifier envs, rubrics, prime-rl configs) for inner models — with real GPU execution as the reward signal, not simulation
audience: ML engineers and researchers exploring automated/agentic training pipelines and meta-RL

title: Coding agents think ahead of time
why: If a coding agent's hidden states already encode whether an edit will parse or pass tests up to ~25 steps before it writes them, you may be able to build cheaper correctness monitors and early-abort signals without waiting for the full test suite.
notes: A logistic-regression probe on the language model's residual-stream hidden states linearly decodes properties of the evolving program: whether the code currently parses, passes its test suite, reduces the number of failing tests, and whether an edit introduces regressions.
Correctness decoding reaches AUC up to 0.83, reported across two models and two benchmarks (the specific models and benchmarks are not named in the abstract).

Headline claim: probes trained to predict outcomes of future edits, before those edits are materialized on disk, perform above chance up to roughly 25 steps in advance; the authors coin this the 'latent programming horizon.'
Claimed external validity: probes transfer across benchmarks without retraining, though the abstract gives no transfer AUC numbers.

Positioned as a mechanistic interpretability study of agentic, multi-step coding, not a benchmark or product; the abstract offers no baseline comparison, causal test, or downstream-utility demonstration.
summary: This arXiv paper (Latent Programming Horizons in Coding Agents) probes what a language model driving a coding agent internally represents about the program it is editing. Using logistic-regression probes on residual-stream hidden states, the authors report that the model linearly encodes whether current code parses, passes tests, reduces failing tests, and introduces regressions, reaching AUC up to 0.83 for correctness across two models and two benchmarks. Their more striking claim is that these representations run ahead of the agent's own actions: probes predict the outcome of future edits, before they are written to disk, above chance up to roughly 25 steps in advance, a phenomenon they name the "latent programming horizon." As evidence of external validity, they report that probes transfer across benchmarks without retraining. The work is framed as motivation for further mechanistic interpretability research on coding agents rather than as a product or benchmark result, and the abstract does not disclose the specific models, benchmarks, or downstream applications.
novelty: Discovery that coding-agent LM residual streams encode future program state up to ~25 steps before those edits are materialized — dubbed the "latent programming horizon" — a mechanistic interpretability finding specific to agentic, multi-step coding settings.
audience: ML researchers and engineers building or studying coding agents / LM-based software-engineering systems.

title: New York becomes first state to impose one-year pause on new AI datacenters
why: If you plan AI infrastructure or capacity, the largest US state just froze new hyperscale datacenter permitting for a year, signaling that grid, water, and utility-bill politics are becoming a hard constraint on where compute can be built.
notes: Gov. Kathy Hochul's executive order (issued Tuesday, July 14, 2026) imposes a one-year statewide pause on state permitting for proposed 'hyperscale' datacenters, defined as electrical capacity above 50 MW.
The order directs state regulators to write new standards covering environmental impact, energy demand, and water usage during the pause.

This is the first US state-level moratorium to take effect: Maine's legislature passed one but its governor vetoed it in April 2026; a small southern California city and then Seattle enacted municipal-level moratoriums earlier.

Hochul acted via executive order rather than signing a broader moratorium bill the NY legislature already passed, which added an environmental impact report plus labor, energy efficiency, and transparency provisions; bill author state Sen. Kristen Gonzalez welcomed the order.

Polling cited: a June 2026 Siena Research Institute poll found 46% of New Yorkers thought the one-year pause would be good and 21% bad; a Heatmap poll found nearly three-quarters of Americans oppose a datacenter being built near their home.
The article does not detail how existing permitted projects are treated, enforcement mechanics, or the executive order's legal durability versus the pending legislation.
summary: New York Governor Kathy Hochul issued an executive order on July 14, 2026, pausing state permitting of new 'hyperscale' datacenters, defined as facilities with more than 50 MW of electrical capacity, for one year, making New York the first US state to enact such a moratorium. Hochul framed the move around rising utility bills, resource depletion, and environmental uncertainty, and directed state regulators to develop standards on environmental impact, energy demand, and water usage. Notably, she used an executive order rather than signing a more comprehensive moratorium bill the state legislature had already passed, which included an environmental impact report and new labor, energy efficiency, and transparency standards; the bill's author, state Senator Kristen Gonzalez, still welcomed the order. The action reflects growing national backlash: Maine's legislature passed a moratorium that its governor vetoed in April 2026, and a southern California city followed by Seattle enacted municipal-level pauses. Cited polling includes a June 2026 Siena survey (46% of New Yorkers view the pause as good, 21% as bad) and a Heatmap poll finding nearly three-quarters of Americans oppose a datacenter near their home.
novelty: First US state-level executive moratorium on hyperscale datacenter construction, bypassing a more comprehensive legislative bill already passed by the NY legislature
audience: Infrastructure/platform engineers and executives planning AI datacenter capacity in the Northeast US

title: Weak-to-Strong Generalization via Direct On-Policy Distillation
why: If RL gains can be transferred from a small cheap model to a larger one as a reusable reward signal, post-training a frontier model no longer requires rerunning expensive rollouts at full scale.
notes: Direct On-Policy Distillation (Direct-OPD) transfers a weak model's RL-induced policy shift, not its final outputs: it compares the post-RL teacher against its own pre-RL reference and treats the log-ratio of their policies as a dense implicit reward.
That signal is applied on the strong student's own on-policy states, which the authors argue avoids inheriting the smaller teacher's capability limitations while still reusing its RL supervision.
Reported result: Qwen3-1.7B improved from 48.3% to 58.3% on AIME 2024 without running sparse-reward RL on the target model, trained in 4 hours on 8 A100 GPUs.
Claimed to outperform step-matched direct RL on the target model and to support sequential composition of multiple policy shifts from different RL runs.
Motivation: RLVR requires the target model to generate many rollouts during training, a cost that scales poorly and makes post-training itself a bottleneck at frontier scale.
Caveats: this is an arXiv preprint; results center on a single small target model (Qwen3-1.7B) and math benchmarks, with no reported comparison against larger targets or peer review.
summary: The paper proposes Direct On-Policy Distillation (Direct-OPD), a weak-to-strong method that reuses the outcome of reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) across model scales rather than repeating expensive rollout-heavy RL on each new strong model. Instead of distilling the post-RL weak teacher directly (which mixes RL gains with the small model's limitations), Direct-OPD computes the log-ratio between the teacher's post-RL policy and its pre-RL reference and uses that as a dense implicit reward, applied on the stronger student's own on-policy states. The authors report that it lifts Qwen3-1.7B from 48.3% to 58.3% on AIME 2024 in 4 hours on 8 A100 GPUs, without running sparse-reward RL on the target model. They claim it beats step-matched direct RL and enables sequential composition of multiple policy shifts. The broader claim is that RL outcomes can be reused as transferable implicit reward signals, not merely as final models to imitate. Evidence is currently limited to a preprint with a narrow experimental setup.
novelty: Using the log-ratio of a weak model's post-RL vs. pre-RL checkpoints as a dense implicit reward signal applied to a stronger model's own on-policy states — transferring RL *policy shift* rather than imitating final outputs
audience: ML researchers and post-training practitioners doing RLVR at scale on large models

title: Murati's Thinking Machines Releases Open-Weights 975B Parameter LLM

This is the first open-weights release from Thinking Machines, the lab founded by former OpenAI CTO Mira Murati.
Caveat: the announcement provides no benchmark numbers, evaluation methodology, license terms, or comparisons to substantiate the 'frontier' and 'well-rounded generalist' claims.
summary: Thinking Machines Lab, the AI company founded by former OpenAI CTO Mira Murati, has released Inkling, its first open-weights model. Inkling is a Mixture-of-Experts model with 975B total parameters and 41B active per token, natively multimodal on input across text, image, and audio, and available to fine-tune. The company advertises a 1M-token context window (64K default, 256K on its 'Tinker' tier), controllable reasoning effort, agentic coding and tool use, and calibrated confidence for forecasting, and describes the model as 'at the open-source frontier for speech.' The announcement is a capabilities overview only and includes no benchmark results, evaluation methodology, license details, or comparative data to support its 'frontier' and 'generalist' positioning. It surfaced with modest early attention (29 points on Hacker News).
