title: S&P downgrades Oracle to BBB- – only one notch above junk level
why: S&P just put a number on the systemic risk everyone senses: roughly half of Oracle's $638B undelivered cloud backlog rides on OpenAI, whose ability to pay depends on the AI boom continuing.
notes: S&P cut Oracle from BBB to BBB- on July 9, 2026, the lowest investment-grade notch (one step above junk), keeping a stable outlook; it had already moved the outlook to 'negative' in July 2025 flagging exactly this scenario.
The agency forecasts a ~$42B free operating cash flow deficit for Oracle's FY2027 (ending May 2027), to be funded with a mix of debt and equity.
Oracle raised FY2027 capex guidance to $90-95B versus S&P's earlier $60B assumption, with the gap attributed to rising GPU and network equipment costs.

About half of Oracle's $638B contractually promised but undelivered service volume is tied to OpenAI, which S&P explicitly labels a 'central credit risk'; a default would leave Oracle holding long-term datacenter leases it cannot easily terminate or reassign, and OpenAI's ability to pay depends on continued AI demand, model leadership, and ongoing outside fundraising.
Oracle cut over 21,000 jobs (~13% of workforce) in the past year, framed as a shift 'from people to machines' to fund AI buildout.

Cloud infrastructure was ~27% of FY2026 revenue and S&P projects ~60% by 2028, but rates Oracle structurally weaker than Microsoft, Google, and Amazon due to greater external-customer dependence, less financial cushion, and emerging competition such as SpaceX renting compute to Anthropic and Alphabet.
summary: S&P Global downgraded Oracle's credit rating from BBB to BBB- on July 9, 2026, the lowest investment-grade tier, citing debt-fueled expansion of its AI datacenter business; the outlook remains stable. The agency forecasts a roughly $42B free operating cash flow deficit for FY2027 as Oracle lifts capex guidance to $90-95B (up from an assumed $60B), attributing the jump to rising GPU and networking costs. S&P's sharpest concern is concentration: about half of Oracle's $638B undelivered contractual backlog is attributable to OpenAI, which it designates a 'central credit risk' because OpenAI's payments hinge on a continuing AI boom and repeated external fundraising, leaving Oracle exposed to hard-to-reassign long-term leases if OpenAI falters. Oracle is transitioning from software vendor to hyperscaler (cloud infrastructure ~27% of FY2026 revenue, projected ~60% by 2028) but is rated structurally weaker than Microsoft, Google, and Amazon, and has cut over 21,000 jobs (~13% of staff) to fund the buildout. The article situates this within broader warnings, including the BIS drawing parallels between debt-financed AI investment and the dot-com bubble and 2008 crisis.
novelty: S&P's explicit quantification of OpenAI as ~50% of Oracle's $638B undelivered contractual backlog and formal designation as a "central credit risk" — the clearest public accounting yet of systemic OpenAI concentration in AI infrastructure
audience: Engineers evaluating AI cloud vendor stability and infrastructure ecosystem concentration risk

title: Is x86 ready to ACE it?
why: If you write or tune ML kernels for CPUs, ACE is the x86 industry's bet on how the next generation of Intel and AMD chips will do matrix math, and this teardown lets you reason about it before any silicon exists.
notes: ACE is a second AMX accelerator type (alongside Sapphire Rapids' TMUL), specified in an x86 Ecosystem Advisory Group whitepaper. It swaps TMUL's inner-product instructions for outer products, fixes tile geometry at 64 bytes x 16 rows, drops complex-number support, and adds FP8. It reuses AMX's existing 8 KB of tile registers.

Key structural change: ACE math instructions take A/B inputs from AVX-512/AVX10 vector registers and accumulate into tile registers, versus TMUL which sources all operands from tile registers. This frees tile capacity for a larger 32x64 accumulator (vs AMX's ~32x48).

The author's cache-traffic model for a 32K x 32K INT8 multiply (35.2 trillion MACs): ACE ~1.5 TB, AMX TMUL ~1.7 TB, SME at 512-bit SVL ~2.0 TB, AVX-512-VNNI ~3.4 TB. General reuse lower bound is 2*M*N*K/sqrt(T), T being accumulator tile size in elements; the ACE register file was not expanded to VEX's full 16 registers, which would make it match a 1024-bit SME implementation.

Dequantization: a new VUNPACKB instruction (ACE/AVX10.3) extracts 2-to-7-bit elements to byte-aligned positions; VPERMB handles up to 6-bit LUTs and VPERMI2B pairs two 512-bit registers for 7-bit inputs. The article argues this software-driven approach is more flexible than SME2's fixed 512-bit ZT0 lookup register plus LUTI2/LUTI4, which only accelerate 2- or 4-bit formats, though SME2 does conversions in one instruction and off the vector register file.

Block scaling for FP8 (OCP Microscaling Formats): ACE adds a 1024-bit BSR0 register, two 512-bit halves (one per outer-product input), each holding four groups of 16 8-bit scale values selected by an immediate. SME uses LSCALE/LSCALE2 fields in FPMR. The author notes neither BSR0 nor FPMR is likely to be register-renamed, so scale updates would serialize all FP8 math on both ISAs; update instructions overwrite the whole register, implying designers expect scale values to change rarely.

Caveat the author stresses: no ACE hardware exists yet; Intel and AMD may target next-gen CPUs. All performance claims are ISA-level speculation, and the piece states hardware implementation quality matters far more than ISA design.
summary: Chips and Cheese analyzes the x86 Ecosystem Advisory Group's whitepaper for ACE, a new AMX accelerator type that complements Intel's existing TMUL matrix unit. ACE replaces inner-product instructions with outer products, fixes tile geometry at 64B x 16 rows, drops complex numbers, adds FP8, and, crucially, sources its A/B inputs from AVX-512 vector registers rather than tile registers, freeing tile capacity for larger accumulators. The author builds a cache-bandwidth model for a 32K x 32K INT8 multiply showing ACE (~1.5 TB) beating AMX TMUL (~1.7 TB), Arm SME at 512-bit SVL (~2.0 TB), and AVX-512-VNNI (~3.4 TB), with savings driven by larger output tiles that reduce input reloads. The piece contrasts ACE's dequantization path (VUNPACKB plus VPERMB/VPERMI2B using wide vectors as lookup tables, flexible for any 2-to-7-bit format) against SME2's fixed ZT0 register and LUTI instructions, and compares block-scaling support (ACE's 1024-bit BSR0 vs Arm's FPMR fields), noting both likely serialize FP8 math on scale updates because neither register is expected to be renamed. The article repeatedly emphasizes that no ACE silicon exists yet, that Intel and AMD may support it in next-gen CPUs, and that hardware implementation quality will matter more than ISA design, making all conclusions provisional.
novelty: First deep technical teardown of the x86 EAG's ACE whitepaper — a new outer-product AMX accelerator — including quantitative cache-bandwidth comparisons against AMX TMUL, AVX-512-VNNI, and Arm SME, all before any hardware ships.
audience: CPU microarchitecture engineers and ML inference systems engineers optimizing for x86 and Arm silicon

title: Running Gemma 4 26B at 5 tokens/SEC on a 13-year-old Xeon with no GPU
why: If you have pre-Haswell enterprise iron gathering dust, this is a concrete recipe and a still-open patch to run a 26B MoE locally at reading speed, plus a clean case study of driving an LLM agent through a real performance-C++ debugging session.
notes: Hardware: dual Xeon E5-2690 v2 (Ivy Bridge, 2013), AVX1 only (no AVX2, no FMA3), DDR3, no GPU, box cost under $300; reported ~5.2 tok/s decode and ~16 tok/s prompt eval on Gemma 4 26B-A4B (MoE) at Q8_0.

Root cause: ik_llama.cpp's graph builder unconditionally emits MOE_FUSED_UP_GATE and FUSED_UP_GATE ops, but the compute dispatcher has no case for them when GGML_USE_IQK_MULMAT is off, so ~240 expert FFN destination tensors per forward pass (30 layers x 8 active experts) were silently left as uninitialized memory.

Symptom signature that cracked it: deterministic at temperature 0, bit-identical across thread counts, no NaNs, but fluent multilingual gibberish; instrumented logits showed a mean of +16 (expected near 0) with ~80% of the 262K vocab at positive logits, pointing to uninitialized-but-small-positive floats rather than random corruption.

Fix is three commits gated behind #if !GGML_USE_IQK_MULMAT: rewrite non-scalar 'scalar' quantize branches as portable loops, and have the graph builder split the fused up_gate_exps tensor via ggml_view_3d, run two ggml_mul_mat_id calls, and recombine with ggml_fused_mul_unary(SILU); AVX2 builds stay bit-identical. Extra cost is two matmul-IDs vs one fused kernel, but the box is memory-bandwidth-bound and the fused kernel was AVX2-only.
Separate unfixed gotcha: --run-time-repack reorders weights into the AVX2-only Q8_0_R8 interleaved layout at startup and re-garbles AVX1 output; the workaround is to drop the flag, not patch it.

Caveats: patch is PR ikawrakow/ik_llama.cpp#2138, open and awaiting maintainer review at publication; the author states plainly he is not a C++ programmer, that the diagnosis and patch came from a Claude instance, and that his role was running experiments and judging correctness. The post is on the author's company blog and doubles as marketing for legacy-code consulting.
summary: The author revives a ~13-year-old, GPU-less HP StoreVirtual box (dual Ivy Bridge Xeon E5-2690 v2, AVX1 only, under $300) to run Google's Gemma 4 26B-A4B mixture-of-experts model at roughly 5.2 tokens/sec decode and ~16 tokens/sec prompt eval using a patched ik_llama.cpp built with GGML_USE_IQK_MULMAT disabled. The fork assumes AVX2/FMA3 as a floor, so it neither built nor ran correctly on the older chip; the visible failure was fluent multilingual gibberish. Root cause was a silent dispatcher fall-through: the graph builder emitted MOE_FUSED_UP_GATE/FUSED_UP_GATE ops the compute path did not handle without IQK, so every forward pass left ~240 expert FFN tensors as uninitialized memory, biasing logits (mean +16, ~80% of the 262K vocab positive). The three-commit fix adds real scalar quantize fallbacks and rewrites the graph builder to emit ops with working non-IQK implementations (ggml_view_3d split, two ggml_mul_mat_id calls, ggml_fused_mul_unary SILU), all gated so AVX2 builds are unchanged; users must also drop --run-time-repack, which garbles output on AVX1. The patch (PR ikawrakow/ik_llama.cpp#2138) was still open and unreviewed at publication. The author openly credits a Claude agent with the diagnosis and patch, framing his contribution as running experiments and recognizing correctness, and the post also promotes his firm's legacy-code work.
novelty: First documented AVX1-only fallback patch for ik_llama.cpp enabling Gemma 4 MoE on pre-Haswell (Ivy Bridge) CPUs, including root-cause diagnosis of a silent dispatcher bug unique to that build configuration
audience: Local inference engineers and systems hackers running LLMs on CPU-only or legacy enterprise hardware

title: SynthDocBench: Controlled Benchmark for Long-Context Visual Document Understanding
why: If your product relies on VLMs to read long, mixed-modality documents, this benchmark argues their headline scores may reflect overfitting to benchmark artifacts rather than robust comprehension, with predictable failures by length and position.
notes: SynthDocBench is a fully synthetic, combinatorially designed benchmark that varies four factors independently: document length, layout structure, modality composition, and question type, so failures can be attributed to specific causes rather than confounded together.

Documents are generated end to end by an LLM pipeline across six layout archetypes, with a 40 percent random override to stop models from exploiting spurious layout-to-answer correlations; the authors claim greater length and structural diversity than existing benchmarks.

Across seven frontier VLMs, the authors report three failure modes: sharp accuracy degradation as document length grows, positional sensitivity, and breakdown of chart comprehension specifically in long-document settings.
Positional finding: the middle third of a document is hardest for 5 of 6 models, and 5 of 6 show a negative Early-to-Late trend, with a steepest single-model decline of 8.3 percentage points.

The authors frame these results as evidence that current models may be overfitting to benchmark artifacts on DocVQA, ChartQA, and MMLongBench-Doc rather than achieving robust long-context visual document understanding.

Caveats: it is a synthetic benchmark (results may not transfer to real documents), the abstract does not name the specific models, absolute accuracy numbers, or generation-pipeline details, and no code or data availability is stated in the provided text.
summary: SynthDocBench is a fully synthetic benchmark for long-context visual document understanding that uses a combinatorial design to vary document length, layout structure, modality composition, and question type independently, enabling controlled attribution of model failures. Documents are generated end to end by an LLM pipeline across six layout archetypes with a 40 percent random override intended to prevent models from exploiting spurious correlations, and the authors claim substantially greater length and structural diversity than DocVQA, ChartQA, and MMLongBench-Doc. Evaluating seven frontier VLMs, the paper reports three failure modes existing benchmarks cannot surface: sharp degradation as documents lengthen, a positional sensitivity in which the middle third is hardest for 5 of 6 models and 5 of 6 show a negative Early-to-Late trend (steepest decline 8.3 percentage points), and breakdown of chart comprehension in long-document settings. The authors argue these results suggest current models may be overfitting to benchmark artifacts rather than achieving robust long-context document understanding. The abstract does not disclose the specific models, absolute scores, or generation details, and the findings are on synthetic rather than real documents.
novelty: Controlled synthetic benchmark isolates three VLM failure modes—length degradation, positional sensitivity, chart comprehension collapse—that existing benchmarks (DocVQA, ChartQA, MMLongBench-Doc) structurally cannot surface.
audience: ML engineers and researchers building, fine-tuning, or evaluating VLMs on long-context document tasks.

title: Google and Epic give up fighting — third-party Android app stores are coming next week
why: After a decade of walled-garden Android, rival app stores will live inside Google Play in the US starting July 22nd, and your app is opted in by default unless you say otherwise.
notes: Google told the court it will begin carrying third-party app stores inside Google Play on Wednesday, July 22nd, 2026, after it and Epic jointly withdrew their motion to modify Judge James Donato's permanent injunction; a July 16th hearing may now be moot.

The underlying injunction (October 2024) requires Google to host rival Android stores within Play for several years and share its entire app catalog with them; the injunction only bars Google from *prohibiting* rival stores, not necessarily obligating it to proactively invite them.

US developers' app and game listings are automatically provided to third-party stores starting July 22nd unless they opt out; Google has launched a Play Catalog Access Program enrollment page for the stores themselves.

Stores get catalog access for a $5,000 annual fee for 'security and policy reviews,' and must meet conditions: US-only distribution, openness to all eligible developers, 'clear, non-discriminatory' trust and safety policies, and no more than 1% of 'install attempts' being malware.

Google and Epic reached a secret $800 million deal as part of a global settlement; Google convinced Epic to back its rejected alternative, a sideloaded 'Registered App Stores' program, which Donato was skeptical of.

This creates a two-track world: stores-within-a-store in the US, versus the sideloaded Registered App Stores model Google plans to roll out elsewhere via a future Android release; broader billing and fee economics remain unclear per The Verge.
summary: The Verge reports that Google and Epic Games jointly withdrew their motion to modify Judge James Donato's antitrust injunction, meaning Google will be forced to carry rival Android app stores inside Google Play in the US and share its full app catalog with them. Google told the court it is ready to begin on July 22nd, 2026, and is already notifying US developers that their listings will be automatically provided to third-party stores unless they opt out. To access the Play catalog, stores must pay a $5,000 annual fee for security and policy reviews and meet requirements including US-only distribution, openness to all eligible developers, non-discriminatory trust and safety policies, and keeping malware below 1% of install attempts. The retreat follows a secret $800 million settlement between the companies and Donato's skepticism toward Google's proposed sideloaded 'Registered App Stores' alternative, which Google now plans to deploy outside the US, producing a two-track global system. Broader questions about billing and distribution economics remain unresolved, per the article.
novelty: Confirmed July 22nd go-live date for stores-within-Google-Play in the US, plus first disclosure of the $5,000 annual catalog-access fee and automatic opt-in for US developers
audience: Android app developers and platform engineers tracking app-store distribution economics

title: Show HN: Juggler – an open-source GUI coding agent, by the creator of JUCE
why: If you dislike babysitting a coding agent through an opaque linear chat, Juggler is a serious attempt to make every tool call, approval, and context item inspectable and editable in a branching tree, from a builder with real framework-design credibility.
notes: Session is a Yjs CRDT document shaped as a tree, not a linear transcript: any node can branch into sub-threads, and you can backtrack, compare, and directly edit structure. The UI renders this in Finder-style Miller columns so tool calls, approvals, thread structure, item properties, and raw context are visible inline rather than buried in collapsibles.

Extension model is the core bet: context item types (read-file, replace-text, bash), LLM loop strategies (plan, research), and slash commands (/clear, /compact) are all JavaScript plugins you can inspect, fork, or replace without rebuilding the app.

Stack is deliberately non-Electron: Go backend with Wails for windowing, HTML/JS UI served by the Go backend, JSDoc types enforced by strict CI linting, and no build step between source and shipped code. Author is credited as the creator of JUCE, the C++ audio framework.

Architecture is a local webserver serving a live collaborative session: the native desktop app, local browser tabs, and remote machines can all attach as clients to the same headless `juggler` server simultaneously. Connects to Claude Code (CLI or API), OpenAI codex/API, Gemini, Ollama, OpenRouter, Z.AI, Deepseek and more.

Licensing is split: core app is AGPLv3-or-later (hosted modifications must be released under AGPLv3), while the extension SDK and bundled extensions are Apache-2.0 so closed-source extensions are allowed. Commercial licensing is available by contacting the author.

Security caveat: the server is localhost-only by default; LAN access (via `p` or `--public`) has no password, so anyone who can reach the address can drive the agent. WAN/internet access is not in the OSS repo and ships only in official binaries from juggler.studio.
summary: Juggler is an open-source GUI coding agent, announced on Hacker News (247 points) by the creator of the JUCE C++ audio framework, aimed at developers who want fine-grained visibility and control over what an LLM does to their codebase. Its central design choice is representing a session as a tree-shaped Yjs CRDT document rather than a linear transcript, allowing sub-thread branching, backtracking, comparison, and direct editing of any node, surfaced through Finder-style Miller columns that expose tool calls, approvals, and raw context. Context item types, LLM loop strategies, and slash commands are all JavaScript plugins that can be inspected, forked, or replaced without rebuilding the app. Technically it runs a Go plus Wails backend (explicitly not Electron) serving an HTML/JS UI with JSDoc-based types and no build step, and it functions as a local webserver so desktop, browser, and remote clients can attach to the same headless server session at once. It connects to Claude Code, OpenAI, Gemini, Ollama, OpenRouter, Z.AI, Deepseek and others. The core is AGPLv3 while the extension SDK and bundled extensions are Apache-2.0; the server is localhost-only by default, LAN access is unauthenticated, and WAN access is reserved for official binaries outside the repo.
novelty: Tree-structured Yjs CRDT session (not linear transcript) + Miller-columns UI for full agent transparency, from JUCE's creator, on a Go+Wails (non-Electron) substrate
audience: Software engineers who want structural control and auditability over AI coding agent sessions, not just a smarter autocomplete

title: The Tower Keeps Rising
why: If your team is scaling agent-assisted development, this names the failure mode you will not see coming: architectural coherence can rot silently while the code keeps compiling and shipping.
notes: Author is Armin Ronacher (creator of Flask and Jinja2), writing from a practitioner vantage rather than as a pundit; the essay is an argument by analogy, not empirical study.

Core claim: large software has never been bottlenecked only by individual coding speed but by how well people coordinate a shared understanding of a system, a 'shared language' of concepts, boundaries, invariants, and ownership that mostly lives in review, conversation, and argument rather than in one written place.

Mechanism: pre-agent friction (reading someone's code, asking questions, cross-team coordination) was partly waste but partly the process that synchronized mental models; agents remove that friction, so each developer can alter any corner of the system via a 'tireless translator' without acquiring the shared model.

Illustrative scenario: dev A has an agent add OAuth, dev B add caching, dev C rebuild the database from first principles and turn the UI pink; each change is reasonable in isolation, compiles, and passes tests, with explanations generated on demand and no human conversation required.

The central inversion vs. the Bruegel/biblical Tower of Babel: at Babel the loss of shared language halts construction, whereas in AI-assisted engineering construction continues after shared understanding has already collapsed, so there is no immediate failure to signal the loss.

Recurring framing: 'agents do not feel pain, only humans do' — agents operate in code where humans would previously have had to involve others, and the missing failure signal ('the tower does not fall') makes the degradation invisible until late.
summary: Armin Ronacher argues that AI coding agents introduce a distinct failure mode in large software projects by removing the coordination friction that once kept developers' mental models in sync. He frames it through Bruegel's Tower of Babel: the real source of the builders' power was a shared language, and losing it stopped construction. In software, the analogous 'shared language' is the common understanding of a system's concepts, boundaries, invariants, and ownership, maintained largely through code review, cross-team questions, and the effort of explaining changes. Agents let each developer alter any part of a codebase without acquiring that shared model, so changes keep landing (compiling, passing tests, with on-demand explanations) even as architectural coherence erodes. Ronacher's key inversion is that, unlike Babel, construction does not stop when shared understanding collapses; the tower keeps rising, which is precisely what makes the loss disorienting and hard to notice. The piece is a conceptual argument from a credible technical voice, not an empirical or measured study.
novelty: Precise articulation of a failure mode unique to AI-assisted development: architectural coherence collapses silently because construction never stops to signal the loss — the inverse of Babel
audience: Senior engineers and engineering managers running multi-team codebases with AI agent adoption

title: @clickhouse/rowbinary: when your library is also a parser compiler
why: If you pull data from ClickHouse into Node at high QPS, this shows a concrete, auditable way to get monomorphized per-query parsers, plus a reusable pattern for shipping a library as a reference implementation an agent forks rather than an API it calls.
notes: The package ships two layers: a conventional library of monomorphizable, type-specific RowBinary read primitives (parseRowBinary, writers, streaming, full-buffer and chunked variants), and a SKILL.md that teaches a coding agent to assemble those primitives into a parser specialized to a query's exact column types. The skill is registered in the package's agents.skills field so agents scanning node_modules find it automatically.

Agent-generated monomorphic parsers beat the composed generic reader by 1.55x (financial ledger, UInt128/Int128), 2.46x (IoT Float64/integers), and 3.41x (fixed-width orders schema) on 50k rows/schema; the win comes from one bounds check per row, reads at constant offsets, baked-in Decimal scale, and a lookup-table UUID formatter instead of per-cell dispatch that defeats V8's inliner.

Correctness is the stated primary motivation over speed: asked to write a RowBinary UUID decoder (two byte-reversed little-endian UInt64 halves) from memory, Sonnet 4.6 got byte order wrong in 3 of 5 runs, each producing plausible-but-corrupt output; Opus 4.8 was 5/5; with the skill loaded every run is correct by construction because the tested primitive sits two calls away.

Generation cost with Claude Sonnet 4.6: ~230k tokens in (mostly prompt-cached; ~28k unique skill footprint per call), ~2.1k out, about $0.20/parser cached, $0.72 uncached upper bound. The generated parser is regular source that a human commits, reviews, tests, and benchmarks; the skill is auditable markdown plus commented TypeScript in the npm tarball.

RowBinary beats the correct JSON path (server-side stringify of wide integers plus client BigInt reparse) 3.3x on Apple M4 Max and 2.5x on a 4-vCPU AMD EPYC 7763; bare JSONEachRow looks closer at 1.8x but silently rounds UInt128, Int128, and UInt64 past Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER to float64. Caveat: on a string-heavy log schema JSONCompactEachRow beats even the optimized reader, and the skill says not to use RowBinary there.

Framed as decomposing a codegen compiler (protoc/flatc/Cap'n Proto shape) into inference-time artifacts: customization becomes a conversation with a model that read the library rather than a fixed matrix of flags. Agent Skills launched as a format October 2025 and became an open standard December 2025; the common pattern is SKILL.md-as-API-guide, while this is SKILL.md-as-reference-to-fork. Smaller models degrade but stay viable with focused sub-agents (Haiku pass rate rose from 52% to 86%).
summary: ClickHouse released @clickhouse/rowbinary, a Node.js reader/writer for its RowBinary formats that ships both as a conventional library of type-specific parsing primitives and as an Agent Skill. Instead of calling a generic, per-cell type-dispatched parser (which pays a bounds check and megamorphic dispatch per cell and defeats V8 inlining), a coding agent reads the bundled SKILL.md and library source and emits a parser monomorphized to a query's exact column types, with constant-offset reads and a single per-row bounds check. ClickHouse reports these generated parsers run 1.5x to 3.4x faster than the composed generic reader across numeric-heavy schemas, cost roughly $0.20 each to generate with Claude Sonnet 4.6 (prompt-cached), and produce byte-identical output that a human still reviews and commits. The company frames correctness, not speed, as the main win: models writing binary decoders from memory silently corrupt data (Sonnet 4.6 got RowBinary UUID byte order wrong in 3 of 5 runs), whereas the skill makes the parser a recombination of official, tested primitives. RowBinary itself is also reported to beat the correct JSON path by 2.1x to 3.3x, though the skill acknowledges JSONCompactEachRow wins on string-heavy schemas. The post positions this as a general "library-as-reference" alternative to building schema-driven codegen compilers, trading a flag matrix and maintainer queue for a markdown file plus an inference call.
novelty: "Library-as-reference" agent skill pattern as compiler alternative: ship SKILL.md so agents generate monomorphized, query-specific parsers instead of calling a generic dispatch loop — framed explicitly as decomposing a codegen compiler into inference-time artifacts
audience: Node.js/TypeScript engineers on high-throughput ClickHouse data paths, and library authors considering agent skill design patterns

title: Codex starts encrypting prompts, uses ciphertext for inference instead
why: If you run or debug multi-agent systems, this is a live example of an encryption change silently destroying your audit trail: OpenAI's Codex CLI now hands ciphertext to the model and keeps no readable copy of what one agent asked another to do.
notes: The regression traces to PR #26210, 'Encrypt multi-agent v2 message payloads,' merged 2026-06-05 and shipping in Codex CLI builds after 0.137.0 with MultiAgentV2 enabled; the report is written from merged code behavior, not a local repro.

InterAgentCommunication::new_encrypted() deliberately sets content to an empty string and stores the payload only in encrypted_content, so no plaintext copy of the delegated task survives at any persistence layer.

to_model_input_item() emits a prefix header plus AgentMessageInputContent::EncryptedContent directly as the recipient model's input; per the issue, the child model receives ciphertext rather than plaintext task text. The issue does not show where/how the model decrypts it.

The opacity spans all three v2 tools (spawn_agent, send_message, followup_task) and every readable surface: rollout history, trace reduction, and the structured communication log, which falls back to logging encrypted_content whenever content is empty.

Proposed fix is a dual-content contract: keep encrypted_content for delivery, add a required non-encrypted task_message audit field in content, size-bounded to match the delegated message, matched to deliveries via ciphertext/IDs rather than plaintext equality. The spawn_agent half is prototyped; send_message and followup_task remain to be done.

This is distinct from issue #26753 (encrypted tool schema returning HTTP 400); this report concerns auditability after the encrypted schema is accepted, and explicitly does not ask to revert encrypted delivery.
summary: A Codex CLI GitHub issue (#28058) reports that OpenAI's MultiAgentV2 inter-agent messaging, changed by PR #26210 (merged 2026-06-05, affecting versions after 0.137.0), removed the human-readable audit trail for delegated subagent tasks. The Rust helper InterAgentCommunication::new_encrypted() stores the message only in encrypted_content and leaves the plaintext content field empty, so rollout history, trace reduction, parent-side debug surfaces, and structured communication logs all fall back to ciphertext. Per the report, to_model_input_item() passes the encrypted payload directly as the recipient model's input across spawn_agent, send_message, and followup_task, meaning basic questions like what task a child agent was given cannot be answered after the fact. The author frames this as an opacity tradeoff, not a request to revert encryption, and proposes a dual-content contract: retain encrypted_content for model delivery while adding a required, size-bounded plaintext audit field persisted locally and never fed to the child model. The spawn_agent portion is already prototyped, with send_message and followup_task still to be updated, plus acceptance criteria requiring regression tests that assert both readable local audit data and encrypted recipient-model input. This is separate from issue #26753, which covers encrypted tool schemas returning HTTP 400.
novelty: OpenAI Codex CLI's MultiAgentV2 now passes encrypted ciphertext as the literal model input for inter-agent messages, with no plaintext retained anywhere in the local audit/rollout trail — a concrete example of opacity tradeoffs in production multi-agent systems
audience: Engineers building or auditing multi-agent AI pipelines, especially those using or evaluating Codex CLI's MultiAgentV2 orchestration

title: SpaceXAI’s Grok programming tool was uploading its users’ entire codebase to cloud storage
why: If you piped a repo through Grok Build's CLI, your gitignored files, purged secrets, and credentials may already be sitting in SpaceXAI's cloud storage, and the "privacy" command the vendor pointed to would not have stopped it.
notes: Cereblab published findings Monday (via The Register) showing the Grok Build CLI packaged and uploaded entire code repositories to Google Cloud, including files it was told not to open and secrets deleted from git history.
As of Monday, Cereblab's tests show SpaceXAI servers returning a 'disable_codebase_upload: true' flag and the codebase upload 'no longer fires'; the article does not say what changed server-side.
SpaceXAI pointed to the CLI's /privacy command as the fix, but Cereblab says /privacy is only a per-session retention toggle, not the control that actually stopped the uploads.

Dr. Lukasz Olejnik (independent researcher, King's College London) called the retention 'excessive,' citing at-risk data including proprietary source code, security vulnerability info, personal data, infrastructure details, and credentials.

Elon Musk claimed on X that all previously uploaded data will be 'completely and utterly deleted' and that 'privacy settings are always respected,' while separately asking users to let SpaceXAI retain data as 'helpful for debugging issues.'
The article states Grok Build retained significantly more data than comparable tools like Claude Code, but provides no methodology or quantified comparison.
summary: The Verge, citing The Register and research published Monday by Cereblab, reports that SpaceXAI's Grok Build AI coding CLI was uploading users' entire code repositories to Google Cloud, including files explicitly excluded from access and secrets that had been deleted from git history. Cereblab says SpaceXAI's servers now return a 'disable_codebase_upload: true' flag and the upload no longer fires as of Monday. SpaceXAI initially pointed users to the CLI's /privacy command as the mitigation, but Cereblab notes that command is only a per-session retention toggle and not the control that stopped the uploads. Independent researcher Dr. Lukasz Olejnik of King's College London called the retention 'excessive,' listing at-risk data such as proprietary source code, security vulnerabilities, personal data, infrastructure details, and credentials. Elon Musk said on X that previously uploaded data will be 'completely and utterly deleted' while also soliciting users to permit data retention for debugging. The article claims Grok Build retained significantly more data than tools like Claude Code but does not detail the comparison methodology.
novelty: Grok Build CLI silently exfiltrated full repos to Google Cloud including gitignored files and secrets purged from git history — and SpaceXAI's own suggested mitigation (/privacy command) was misrepresented: it's a per-session toggle, not the actual fix
audience: Software engineers using AI coding CLI tools and security engineers evaluating data retention risks of agentic dev tooling

title: Measuring Input Latency on Linux: X11 vs. Wayland, VRR, and DXVK
why: If you tune Linux for low-latency gaming, this puts numbers on the folklore: native Wayland is essentially as fast as X11, and the real latency killer is XWayland, not the compositor itself.
notes: The author built a custom photosensor + microcontroller device (loosely based on OSLTT, later borrowing from m2p-latency and Open-Source-LDAT) that measures true end-to-end latency from USB mouse click to a screen brightness change, using Diabotical (DX11) via Proton with a HUD white box bound to left click.
X11 beat native Wayland in every pairing but only by 0.14 to 0.22 ms, which the author argues is far too small to explain the widespread 'Wayland feels off' perception.

XWayland (running without PROTON_ENABLE_WAYLAND=1) was the largest single penalty: +3.13 ms vs native Wayland in the plain case (8.06 ms vs 4.93 ms), more than all other measured effects combined, shifting the entire distribution rather than adding occasional bad frames.
VRR was faster in every pairing (0.26 to 0.45 ms) and flattened the p95-p5 spread from 2.6 to 3.0 ms down to 2.1 to 2.2 ms, consistent with frames scanning out when ready.

The dxvk-low-latency fork (PROTON_DXVK_LOWLATENCY=1, netborg, now in proton-cachyos) saved 0.10 to 0.29 ms capped and 0.84 ms uncapped by keeping GPU utilization at 95 to 97% instead of 100%, costing ~45 FPS (715 to 670); in the XWayland case it recovered 2.11 ms.

Caveats: results are best-case (stable FPS at cap, CPU-bound, static scene) on one specific rig (Ryzen 7 5800X3D, RTX 4070 SUPER, 500 Hz QD-OLED at 1440p, CachyOS kernel 7.1.3, NVIDIA 610.43.03, KDE Plasma 6.7.2); absolute numbers will differ, but the relative gains should roughly transfer, and combining X11 + VRR + low-latency beat plain Wayland by 0.72 ms median.
summary: A competitive-FPS Linux gamer built a custom light-sensor and microcontroller rig to measure true end-to-end input latency (USB click to on-screen brightness change) and used it to test three contested optimizations: X11 vs Wayland, VRR, and the dxvk-low-latency Proton fork. The data shows X11 is only 0.14 to 0.22 ms faster than native Wayland, far too little to explain the common perception that Wayland feels laggy. The dominant penalty is XWayland, which added 3.13 ms in the plain case (more than every other effect combined) and shifted the whole distribution; running natively via PROTON_ENABLE_WAYLAND=1 avoids it. VRR reduced latency by 0.26 to 0.45 ms and tightened the distribution, while dxvk-low-latency saved 0.10 to 0.29 ms capped and 0.84 ms uncapped by holding GPU utilization at 95 to 97% at a cost of about 45 FPS. The author notes results are best-case and hardware-specific (Ryzen 7 5800X3D, RTX 4070 SUPER, 500 Hz 1440p QD-OLED, CachyOS), but cites two independent projects (m2p-latency, Open-Source-LDAT) reaching the same conclusion that native Wayland matches X11 and XWayland should be avoided.
novelty: Empirical hardware-sensor measurements quantifying XWayland's 3+ ms latency penalty and disproving the "native Wayland is intrinsically high latency" belief with controlled data
audience: Linux systems and graphics stack engineers making display compositor and driver decisions

title: Microsoft Patches a Record 570 Security Flaws
why: AI-assisted vulnerability discovery is inflating patch volumes and undermining the human-tuned risk ratings engineers rely on to triage: a model just produced working exploits for 13 of 14 bugs Microsoft judged unlikely to be exploited.
notes: July 2026 Patch Tuesday fixed at least 570 flaws, nearly triple the prior month's record; ~60 rated critical, 3 zero-days actively exploited (two are elevation-of-privilege, among ~250 EoP fixes, plus CVE-2026-56155 in AD FS and CVE-2026-56164 in SharePoint).
Microsoft EVP Pavan Davuluri (July 9 blog) explicitly attributes the higher update volume to AI finding 'more issues, faster, across more code.'

CVE-2026-48561: RCE in Microsoft Copilot, CVSS 9.6; Microsoft says an attacker can host a malicious site that makes Edge for Android auto-send crafted prompts to Copilot, executing code over the network without authentication (flagged by Action1's Jack Bicer).

Tenable's Satnam Narang argues the human-centric exploitability index is failing: this month's SharePoint zero-day was rated 'less likely' yet hit CISA's KEV list on July 1; Anthropic's Red Team reports its Mythos Preview model produced PoC exploits for 13 of 14 vulns rated 'Exploitation Less Likely/Unlikely.'
CVE-2026-50661: BitLocker security-feature bypass exposing encrypted data with physical access; publicly documented but no known exploitation per Microsoft.

Ivanti's Chris Goettl notes broader cadence increases: Adobe moving to twice-monthly bulletins (2nd/4th Tuesdays, also citing AI), plus Cisco, Mozilla, Oracle shipping more often, and Google's June 2026 batches exceeding 900 fixes; Krebs advises backing up and delaying installs given stability risk.
summary: Microsoft's July 2026 Patch Tuesday fixed at least 570 vulnerabilities, nearly triple the prior month's record, including roughly 60 critical bugs and three zero-days already exploited in the wild. Microsoft EVP Pavan Davuluri attributes the surge to AI accelerating vulnerability discovery across more code, and Adobe, Cisco, Mozilla, Oracle and Google are cited as also increasing patch cadence (Google exceeded 900 fixes in June 2026). Notable individual flaws include CVE-2026-48561, a CVSS 9.6 RCE in Microsoft Copilot exploitable via a malicious site that makes Edge for Android auto-send prompts, and CVE-2026-50661, a BitLocker bypass requiring physical access. Security researchers argue the record volume signals a deeper problem: Tenable's Satnam Narang notes Microsoft's exploitability index is human-centric and increasingly unreliable, pointing to a SharePoint zero-day rated 'less likely' that CISA added to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities list, and to Anthropic's Red Team finding that its Mythos Preview model built proof-of-concept exploits for 13 of 14 bugs rated 'Exploitation Less Likely' or 'Unlikely.' Krebs recommends backing up and delaying installation given the stability risk of such a large batch. The claims about AI-driven discovery and the Anthropic model results are attributed to Microsoft and researchers rather than independently verified in the piece.
novelty: Record 570-patch Patch Tuesday attributed to AI-aided discovery; Anthropic's Mythos Preview model produced working PoC exploits for 13/14 vulnerabilities Microsoft rated "Exploitation Less Likely" — directly invalidating Microsoft's own exploitability index.
audience: Security engineers and sysadmins responsible for Windows fleet patching and vulnerability triage.

title: ABot-N1: Toward a General Visual Language Navigation Foundation Model
why: If the results hold, ABot-N1 shows that splitting embodied navigation into a slow Chain-of-Thought reasoner and a fast waypoint controller, joined by a pixel-space goal, can beat monolithic observation-to-action policies while staying interpretable.
notes: Architecture is a slow-fast decoupling: a slow vision-language reasoner does explicit Chain-of-Thought and emits a 'pixel goal' (image-space anchor points); a fast action expert consumes both the textual cues and pixel guidance to generate continuous waypoints at native control frequency.
The pixel goal is pitched as a universal interface across five task types: point-goal, object-goal, POI-goal, instruction-following, and person-following.
Claimed results: POI arrival up 35.0 points to 77.3%, plus 95.4% (indoor) and 92.9% (outdoor) success rates on complex scenes; the authors call these new state of the art.

Design motivation is fixing two failure modes the authors attribute to monolithic policies: coordinate drift and poor handling of long-tail semantics, plus the opacity of black-box observation-to-action mappings.
New Point-Goal and POI-Goal benchmarks for urban-scale navigation are released open source alongside the paper.
Caveat: this is an arXiv abstract only; baselines, evaluation protocol, ablations, and model/data scale are not provided in the text, so the state-of-the-art claims are unverified here.
summary: ABot-N1 is proposed as a step toward a general Visual Language Navigation foundation model that decouples cognition from control using a slow-fast architecture guided by dual visual-language signals. A slow vision-language reasoner performs explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning and outputs a compact set of image-space anchor points (a 'pixel goal'), which serves as a universal interface across point-goal, object-goal, POI-goal, instruction-following, and person-following tasks; a fast action expert then combines textual cues and pixel guidance to produce continuous waypoints at native control frequency. The authors position this against monolithic observation-to-action policies, which they say suffer from coordinate drift, poor long-tail semantics, and a lack of interpretability. They report state-of-the-art results, including a 35.0-point gain in urban-scale POI arrival (to 77.3%) and success rates of 95.4% indoor and 92.9% outdoor, with claimed robustness across object-reaching, person-following, and instruction-following. New open-source Point-Goal and POI-Goal benchmarks are released. The claims come from the paper's abstract and lack accompanying methodology, baselines, or ablation detail in the provided text.
novelty: Slow-fast architecture decoupling CoT-based VLM reasoning (producing a pixel-space "goal anchor") from a fast action expert that generates continuous waypoints — rather than monolithic observation-to-action policy
audience: ML researchers and engineers building embodied agents, robotics navigation systems, or multimodal reasoning pipelines
